EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance brought a general two to four inch snowfall to the entire region. As the storm departed, lake effect snow developed off Lakes Erie and Ontario in the colder, westerly flow. The event was a short lived one���mainly in a twelve- hour period from Monday (28th) morning through the pre-dawn hours of Tuesday.  The snows were very disruptive in the Buffalo area during the afternoon commute as whiteout conditions prevailed as 4 to 10 inches of snow falling in just a few hours.  Off Lake Erie, specific snowfall amounts included: Alden 11 inches; Glenwood 10 inches; West Seneca 9 inches; Orchard Park and Elma 8 inches; Dunkirk and Warsaw 7 inches; and Buffalo 3 to 9 inches (from north to south). Off Lake Ontario, specific snowfall amounts included: Copenhagen 14 inches; Beaver Falls 11 inches; Watertown and Lacona 10 inches; Black River 8 inches; and Scriba, Carthage and Palermo 7 inches.

Primary low pressure traveled from the Great Lakes across the New York/Canadian border on January 7th. Meanwhile, a coastal low slowly developed south of New England during the late afternoon of the 7th and eventually intensified as it moved into the Gulf of Maine during the night. ||Snow overspread northern New York during the early morning hours of the 7th and continued until midnight on the 8th. Total snowfall accumulations were generally 6 to 9 inches with a brief period of sleet mixing with the snow at times.

Lake effect snows developed off Lakes Erie and Ontario in a well-aligned, cold, unstable west to southwest flow.  Strong winds not along allowed the snow bands to extend well inland, but produced significantly reduced visibilities in blowing and drifting snow. The Lake Erie snows produced significant accumulations well into the Rochester metro area. Off Lake Erie, the event began in a southwest across northern Erie and southern Niagara counties, and then settled south into southern Erie and Wyoming counties by mid morning Thursday (10th) after dropping 2 to 4 inches. A short wave then approached and lifted the band northward for several hours during the day across the metro Buffalo area and across Genesee and Monroe counties, then settled slowly south and stalled out for many hours during the evening 10th with very intense snowfall rates across the Buffalo south towns and Wyoming county. This band also dropped down across the lakeshore areas of Chautauqua county with intense rates with major implications on the NYS Thruway. The NYS thruway was closed between Dunkirk and the PA line with hundreds of motorists stranded. The activity dropped further south and set up across the southern tier counties for much of Friday (11th) before drier air advected in during the evening. The activity weakened and ended by midnight. Specific snowfall amounts included: Springville 27 inches; Perrysburg 24 inches; Colden and Arcade 23 inches; Warsaw 22 inches; East Aurora and Batavia 17 inches; West Seneca 13 inches; and Buffalo and Rochester 3 to 9 inches (from north to south).|Off Lake Ontario, a similar scenario ensued, several hours later. The band initially set up in the Watertown area Thursday morning and slowly settled south later Thursday into early Friday (11th) with intense rates across southern Jefferson and central Lewis counties. The band then settled further into southern Lewis and Oswego counties later Friday as winds veered a bit further before weakening Saturday morning as the drier air advected in. Specific snowfall amounts included: Highmarket 40 inches; Port Leyden 37 inches; Lowville 30 inches; Redfield 28 inches; Copenhagen 21 inches; Carthage 15 inches; and Watertown 11 inches.

Deep low pressure strengthened as it moved from near Chicago to Quebec. As the system passed to the north, winds shifted to southwest and increased to storm force. The combination of the wind shift, rapid strengthening of the wind speed and the long fetch up the length of Lake Erie produced a seiche on the lake. Water levels rose over six feet in just a few hours.  The Lake Erie water level crested at Buffalo at 7.78 feet. Flooding was reported along the lake shore from Angola to Buffalo���especially along Route 5. The Buffalo Coast Guard was forced to seek safe harbor for its fleet, including using the Black Rock Lock to shelter vessels.

The winter lake effect season's second storm was a marginal one and restricted mainly to snows off Lake Ontario. It began following a cold front as a westerly flow set off a band of fairly heavy lake snows by midday Wednesday (16th) across portions of northern Oswego and southern Lewis counties. The flow veered to northwest Wednesday evening and snow bands shifted south across Oswego county. A good streamer off Georgian Bay reached western Lake Ontario and was enhanced as it crossed the lake right across the Rochester metro area which received several inches of snow during the evening. Another lake snow band developed over eastern Lake Ontario and dropped several inches as it crossed western Oswego county on a north-northwest flow.   This event was relatively minor with few affects. It did drop several inches on greater Rochester but it was during the mid and late evening with minimal traffic impact. Heavier snows were restricted to a small area in western Oswego county. Specific snowfall amounts included: Scriba 15 inches; Minetto 13 inches; Palermo 10 inches; and Martville 8 inches. The Rochester metro are received between four and seven inches.

Following the passage of a strong cold front, strong west winds developed.  Peak gusts ranged from around 45 mph to 55 mph and caused scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds downed a tree branch on a woman in the Bronx, who was taken to a hospital after receiving a minor injury to her head.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the passage of a strong cold front, strong west winds developed.  Peak gusts ranged from around 45 mph to 55 mph and caused scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds downed trees and tree branches which resulted in scattered power outages.  Most of LIPA's 20,000 power outages occurred in the town of North Hempstead, where more than 11,000 outages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the passage of a strong cold front, strong west winds developed.  Peak gusts ranged from around 45 mph to 55 mph and caused scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds downed trees and tree limbs and caused scattered power outages.  Around 11 am EDT, police said that a tree fell on a moving car travelling west on Route 17A in Warwick.  A man and woman suffered minor injuries when their car overturned.  Orange and Rockland Utilities reported power outages in the town of Tuxedo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air following a frontal passage set off lake effect snows early on Friday 16th. Initially, the activity off Lake Erie focused on the Chautauqua ridge on a west wind, with several inches Friday morning, but winds backed slightly during the midday and afternoon, and a decent lake plume lifted well to the north into Buffalo's South Towns and even up into central Erie county. Lake Erie had a large amount of ice but the snow apparently formed off some open water north of Long Point. Over 6 fell in this narrow strip with plenty of blowing snow and near whiteout conditions just to the south of the city which snarled the evening rush hour. The activity weakened during the evening and lifted north across the Buffalo metro area and dropped a quick 1-3 inches early Saturday before dissipating. Reported snowfalls included: 12 inches at Perrsyburg; 10 inches at Elma, 9 inches in East Aurora; and 7 inches at Alden.|Off Lake Ontario, the activity was stronger as ice was not a factor. Bands developed early Friday over northern Cayuga and southwest Oswego counties and gradually meandered northward during Friday and Friday night, with several intense bands setting up across the area from Pulaski to Watertown. This continued through much of Saturday before weakening during the afternoon in the Watertown area, but not before nearly two feet fell. Snowfalls totals included: 29 inches at Pulaski; 21 inches at Watertown; and 12 inches in Carthage and Oswego.

Low pressure moved out of the southern Plains northeast across the Tennessee Valley reaching the southern New England Coast on the 28th. Light precipitation developed ahead of an approaching warm front during Sunday evening, January 4th. With surface temperatures initially remaining below freezing, the precipitation began as a mix of sleet and freezing rain across the southern Adirondacks, western and central Mohawk Valley, and across the Lake George and Saratoga region. The precipitation then changed to plain rain and drizzle by early Monday morning on January 5th.||Ice accretion from freezing rain was generally one tenth of an inch or less. However, this light icing led to locally hazardous travel conditions late Sunday evening into early Monday morning.

Arctic air following another cold front produced lake effect snows off Lake Ontario during Saturday evening 24th. A southwest flow set up intense bands across Jefferson County which dropped close to a foot of snow by Sunday morning in the Watertown area. The bands settled south into southern Jefferson and northern Lewis counties for much of Sunday, with rates of 2-3 inches per hour from Adams to Barnes Corners and Hooker. A weak upper disturbance disrupted the bands Sunday night but they still dropped several inches down into the Oswego area overnight into Monday morning. The activity rejuvenated for awhile Monday and lifted north into Jefferson County before weakening Monday night. Reported snowfall totals included: 38 inches at Barnes Corners; 27 inches at Hooker, 24 inches at Adams, 12 to 16 inches in Watertown; and 11 inches in Oswego.

Arctic air following a frontal passage set off lake effect snows early on Friday 16th. Initially, the activity off Lake Erie focused on the Chautauqua ridge on a west wind, with several inches Friday morning, but winds backed slightly during the midday and afternoon, and a decent lake plume lifted well to the north into Buffalo's South Towns and even up into central Erie county. Lake Erie had a large amount of ice but the snow apparently formed off some open water north of Long Point. Over 6 fell in this narrow strip with plenty of blowing snow and near whiteout conditions just to the south of the city which snarled the evening rush hour. The activity weakened during the evening and lifted north across the Buffalo metro area and dropped a quick 1-3 inches early Saturday before dissipating. Reported snowfalls included: 12 inches at Perrsyburg; 10 inches at Elma, 9 inches in East Aurora; and 7 inches at Alden.|Off Lake Ontario, the activity was stronger as ice was not a factor. Bands developed early Friday over northern Cayuga and southwest Oswego counties and gradually meandered northward during Friday and Friday night, with several intense bands setting up across the area from Pulaski to Watertown. This continued through much of Saturday before weakening during the afternoon in the Watertown area, but not before nearly two feet fell. Snowfalls totals included: 29 inches at Pulaski; 21 inches at Watertown; and 12 inches in Carthage and Oswego.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major thaw and additional rainfall resulted in rapid snowmelt and runoff resulting in several area creeks exceeding their flood stage. Two to five inches of water in the snowpack quickly melted as temperatures climbed into the upper 50s and 60s.  Ellicott Creek at Williamsville (flood stage 8 feet) crested at 8.3 feet. Some flooding occurred along the creek from Lancaster through Cheektowaga to Williamsville. Tonawanda Creek at Batavia (flood stage 9 feet) crested at 10.7 feet and at Rapids (flood stage 12 feet) crested at 14.38 feet. Flooding occurred and/or roads were closed in Batavia, near Kibbe Park, and in Amherst, Clarence and Newstead. Black Creek at Churchville (flood stage 6 feet) crested at 7.64 feet. Some backyard and basement flooding occurred from Churchville to Chili. Oatka Creek at Garbutt (flood stage 6 feet) crested at 7.27 feet. Basement flooding was reported in Scottsville and Garbutt and some low lying agricultural land was inundated.

The lake effect event began as an upslope situation, as wraparound moisture pinwheeled around a departing storm system with snows increasing during the afternoon of the 19th. The snows amounted to several inches during Thursday evening before evolving into more pure lake effect overnight and most of Friday. The activity was most pronounced off the open waters of Lake Ontario and settled south Friday night as winds veered. The activity wound down on Saturday as high pressure built into the region. Totals topped two feet over portions of Oswego county. Specific snowfall amounts included: 35 inches at Redfield; 29 inches at Lacona; 27 inches at West Monroe; 25 inches at Palermo; 24 inches at Fulton; 22 inches at Barnes Corners; and 18 inches at Fair Haven.

Low pressure east of Lake Ontario moved to the New England Coast. Colder air behind the low resulted in a general snowfall of 4 to 6 inches across the Eastern Lake Ontario Region. A narrow band of lake enhanced snow set up over Oswego County producing a swath of nine inch snowfall reports.

Low pressure drifted across the lower Great Lakes. Scattered thunderstorms developed in the unsettled conditions. One of these storms grew to produce three-quarter inch diameter hail. The amount of hail that fell covered the ground with a half- to one-inch depth.
Thunderstorms accompanied the passage of a cold front during the overnight hours. A thunderstorm that crossed northern Cayuga County dropped three-quarter inch hail at Sterling.

A bitterly cold air mass spread across much of east central New York and adjacent western New England during Friday January 16th.||Widespread subzero temperatures were recorded across the region, with temperatures as low as -32 F recorded at Indian Lake in Hamilton County, and -30 F in Speculator.||In addition, some wind added to the extreme cold across portions of the southern Adirondacks and eastern Catskills, with wind chills of -20 to -25 F.

A cold west to southwest flow ahead of a slow moving upper level disturbance brought some general light snow which was enhanced by the lakes during the morning of the 30th; this activity remained in place through the day, amounting to 2 to 4 inches. A single intense streamer formed over Lake Ontario and extended across Oswego County much of the night before dissipating during the morning of the 31st as it moved south. It remained over one spot only a few hours as it was transitional, but was intense enough to drop over a foot of snow on western Oswego County. Specific reports included: 14 inches at Hannibal and 7 to 11 inches as Oswego.

Light precipitation developed ahead of an approaching warm front during Sunday evening, January 4th. With surface temperatures initially remaining below freezing, the precipitation began as a mix of sleet and freezing rain across the southern Adirondacks, western and central Mohawk Valley, and across the Lake George and Saratoga region. The precipitation then changed to plain rain and drizzle by early Monday morning on January 5th.||Ice accretion from freezing rain was generally one tenth of an inch or less. However, this light icing led to locally hazardous travel conditions late Sunday evening into early Monday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along an elevated warm front producing strong damaging winds. Oswego county suffered the brunt of the storms with downed trees and power lines. Just outside of Palermo, up to 30 trees were downed and a garage was damaged.

Thunderstorms developed along an elevated warm front producing strong damaging winds. Oswego county suffered the brunt of the storms with downed trees and power lines. Just outside of Palermo, up to 30 trees were downed and a garage was damaged.

An unusually strong low pressure system crossed southern Ontario and brought strong winds to western New York. Winds gusts were measured between 50 and 55 mph at Buffalo and Niagara Falls. At Rochester airport a gust of 56 mph was measured. The windy conditions in Rochester interrupted the annual Lilac festival. A large tent erected for the graduation ceremonies at Nazareth College was blown down by the wind. Four people (three maintenance workers and one guest) suffered minor injuries in the collapse. The funneling effects of Lake Erie also resulted in downed trees and power lines across parts of Chautauqua county. In Cherry Creek, a large tree fell onto a house and two cars. In Falconer, a trailer was damaged by a downed tree.

Strong thunderstorms accompanied the passage of a cold front during the afternoon hours. The thunderstorms produced hail up to one inch in diameter in Wayne county and strong winds that downed trees and power lines in Jefferson and Lewis counties. The annual Military Appreciation Day at Fort Drum had to be cut short for safety reasons. The winds there blew away a large inflatable slide.

The passage of a strong upper level disturbance allowed strong wind gusts to develop across portions of the southern Adirondacks and Schoharie Valley during Sunday afternoon May 31st. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph were observed, and resulted in some downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and wires were reported down in the vicinity of Corinth and the Great Sacandaga Reservoir due to strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached from the west Sunday afternoon and evening on May 24th, preceded and accompanied by scattered thunderstorms, some of which contained damaging wind gusts and large hail. In addition, locally heavy rain produced isolated flash flooding near Wappingers Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four feet of standing water was reported on State Route 55 near Lagrangeville due to flash flooding from locally heavy rainfall.

In Webster, a tree fell on a house. Hail, up to 1 inch in diameter, was reported at the Buffalo Airport and in Harris Hill and Clarence Center.

Thunderstorms preceded and accompanied a cold front moving across the region during the late morning and afternoon hours. The thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines. Damage was reported in Silver Creek, Busti, and Hanover. In Adams Center, the winds blew down a storage shed. In Livingston county, a thunderstorm produced nickel-size hail just north of Dansville. The thunderstorms also produced heavy downpours that resulted in flash flooding over parts of the higher terrain in southern Erie and Cattaraugus counties. In the town of Collins, Route 39 was closed. Numerous roads were flooded and some damaged in Perrysburg, Cattaraugus county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monthly rainfall well above average during the month of June caused significant crop damage on the farms of the north and south forks of Suffolk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of wet weather caused significant crop damage which lead to lost sales in the hundreds of thousands of dollars. The unofficial monthly rainfall total at the historical climate site in Bridgehampton, NY was 8.42 inches which is 4.75 inches above normal for the month.

Damage was reported in Silver Creek, Busti, and Hanover. In Adams Center, the winds blew down a storage shed. In Livingston county, a thunderstorm produced nickel-size hail just north of Dansville. The thunderstorms also produced heavy downpours that resulted in flash flooding over parts of the higher terrain in southern Erie and Cattaraugus counties. In the town of Collins, Route 39 was closed. Numerous roads were flooded and some damaged in Perrysburg, Cattaraugus county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms winds downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low spawned thunderstorms across portions of the Lower Hudson Valley. The storms were prolific hail producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to penny size hail was reported by a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The thunderstorm that produced a tornado beginning in South Valley continued moving west across Cattaraugus county. It produced another tornado that first touched down at 715 PM EDT on Rice Brook Road in Allegany State Park and continued until lifting at 720 PM EDT at Parkside Drive in Limestone. Most of the damage occurred in heavily forested areas with notable tree damage in the Rice Brook area of the Park. This tornado was ranked an EF1 with a path length of 2.2 miles and a maximum width of 100 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The thunderstorm that produced a tornado beginning in South Valley continued moving west across Cattaraugus county. It produced another tornado that first touched down at 715 PM EDT on Rice Brook Road in Allegany State Park and continued until lifting at 720 PM EDT at Parkside Drive in Limestone. Most of the damage occurred in heavily forested areas with notable tree damage in the Rice Brook area of the Park. This tornado was ranked an EF1 with a path length of 2.2 miles and a maximum width of 100 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved slowly east across Pennsylvania during Thursday June 18th, producing widespread rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches across eastern New York, with the highest amounts occurring across portions of Ulster County. This heavy rainfall resulted in some minor flooding of small streams, as well as in low lying poor drainage areas within Ulster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall led to the closure of several roadways in Ulster County due to minor flooding. Some of these roads included, but were not limited to, a portion of Ulsterville Road in the Town of Shawangunk, where a 100 yard portion of the road was reportedly underwater, Jansen Road in the Town of Shawangunk, Springtown Road between Dug Road and Kleine Kill Drive in the Town of New Paltz, and Kyserike Road in the Town of Rochester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a weakening cold front approaching from the west, and an unstable air mass in place, led to the development of isolated thunderstorms across portions of eastern New York during Thursday evening June 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down on Grandview Avenue in Catskill due to strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of an upper level low pressure system, extreme instability in place, along with a cold front approaching from the west, triggered numerous thunderstorms across eastern New York during Tuesday afternoon and evening on June 30th, some of which became severe.||In addition, some thunderstorms redeveloped over the same areas, producing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down approximately 5 miles north northeast of Kingston due to strong thunderstorm winds.

In Wayne county, hail accompanied the storms with nickel-size hail being reported in Rose.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moving across Cattaraugus county produced a tornado. The tornado initially touched at 650 PM EDT on near Guernsey-Hollow Road in the town of South Valley in the southwest part of the county and moved across the Onoville Marina on Quaker Lake, continuing west across Allegany State Park, lifting near Route 1 in Red House at 710 PM EDT. Most of the property damage from the tornado was within in the Onoville Marina where several boats were overturned. A mobile trailer was also overturned there while several other trailers were pushed off their supports and numerous awnings were destroyed. In Allegany State Park and along Guernsey Hollow Road hundreds of trees were knocked over. Most of the damage occurred in heavily forested areas. There was notable tree damage near the Cain Hollow Campground. The tornado was rated an EF1 with a length of 13 miles and a maximum width of 100 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moving across Cattaraugus county produced a tornado. The tornado initially touched at 650 PM EDT on near Guernsey-Hollow Road in the town of South Valley in the southwest part of the county and moved across the Onoville Marina on Quaker Lake, continuing west across Allegany State Park, lifting near Route 1 in Red House at 710 PM EDT. Most of the property damage from the tornado was within in the Onoville Marina where several boats were overturned. A mobile trailer was also overturned there while several other trailers were pushed off their supports and numerous awnings were destroyed. In Allegany State Park and along Guernsey Hollow Road hundreds of trees were knocked over. Most of the damage occurred in heavily forested areas. There was notable tree damage near the Cain Hollow Campground. The tornado was rated an EF1 with a length of 13 miles and a maximum width of 100 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in a southerly flow of warm, moist, unstable air. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail. Trees and wires were downed. In Webster, a tree fell on a house. Hail, up to 1 inch in diameter, was reported at the Buffalo Airport and in Harris Hill and Clarence Center.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees in the City of Rochester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The thunderstorm that produced a tornado in Genesee county earlier continued to move northeast across the region during the late afternoon hours and spawned another tornado in the village of Hilton in Monroe county. Initial touchdown occurred at 555 PM EDT on the southern edge of the village, just west of Hilton Parma Road and continued in a northeast direction for approximately a mile and a half. The damage from this tornado was primarily tree damage and broken outdoor furniture, although one home on Hillside Drive did sustain minor structural damage. The tornado was rated an EF0 with wind speeds peaking around 75 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The thunderstorm that produced a tornado in Genesee county earlier continued to move northeast across the region during the late afternoon hours and spawned another tornado in the village of Hilton in Monroe county. Initial touchdown occurred at 555 PM EDT on the southern edge of the village, just west of Hilton Parma Road and continued in a northeast direction for approximately a mile and a half. The tornado was rated an EF0 with wind speeds peaking around 75 mph.

The lake effect event began as an upslope situation, as wraparound moisture pinwheeled around a departing storm system with snows increasing during the afternoon of the 19th. The snows amounted to several inches during Thursday evening before evolving into more pure lake effect overnight and most of Friday. The activity was most pronounced off the open waters of Lake Ontario and settled south Friday night as winds veered. The activity wound down on Saturday as high pressure built into the region. Totals topped two feet over portions of Oswego county. Specific snowfall amounts included: 35 inches at Redfield; 29 inches at Lacona; 27 inches at West Monroe; 25 inches at Palermo; 24 inches at Fulton; 22 inches at Barnes Corners; and 18 inches at Fair Haven.

A strengthening low pressure system tracked northeast into the eastern Great Lakes by Sunday morning February 22nd, and into the Canadian Maritimes by Monday morning February 23rd.||A cold front associated with this low approached eastern New York and western New England during Sunday, preceded and accompanied by snow and rain. A period of moderate to locally heavy snowfall occurred across portions of the southwest Adirondacks, as well as across the upper Hudson Valley region, beginning early Sunday morning, and ending by early Monday morning. Snowfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches fell within these regions, although locally higher amounts of up to 10 inches fell within the southern Adirondacks.

A massive convective complex moved from lower Michigan across southern Ontario near Toronto then dove southeast across the Niagara Frontier and Western Southern Tier. Damage reports were fairly widespread across the area with downed power lines, limbs and trees. Power outages resulted from the downed power lines. Several homes and automobiles were damaged by felled trees. The heavy rains that accompanied the thunderstorms produced some flash flooding in Chautauqua County. Small creeks overflowed and parking lots were flooded in Jamestown. In Lakewood, numerous roads and viaducts were flooded and closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed by thunderstorm winds.

A massive convective complex moving south across the region produced a tornado in Allegany county.  The initial touchdown occurred in the northwest corner of New Hudson near White Meeting House Road. Final touchdown occurred on Jackson Hill in Cuba. In addition to trees being downed...a 100' communications tower collapsed. Several homes sustained minor structural damage from the tornado. Several others, and several automobiles, were damaged by felled trees. There are no known injuries due to the tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Survey confirmed tornado touchdown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive convective complex moved from lower Michigan across southern Ontario near Toronto then dove southeast across the Niagara Frontier and Western Southern Tier. Damage reports were fairly widespread across the area with downed power lines, limbs and trees. Power outages resulted from the downed power lines. Several homes and automobiles were damaged by felled trees. The heavy rains that accompanied the thunderstorms produced some flash flooding in Chautauqua County. Small creeks overflowed and parking lots were flooded in Jamestown. In Lakewood, numerous roads and viaducts were flooded and closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive convective complex moved from lower Michigan across southern Ontario near Toronto then dove southeast across the Niagara Frontier and Western Southern Tier. This followed an earlier round of strong thunderstorms and heavy rains earlier in the day. Damage from the thunderstorm winds was widespread across the Niagara Frontier and Western Southern Tier.  Trees as large as two to three feet in diameter were downed. Power outages were scattered throughout the region as the winds downed power lines.|The unprecedented heavy rains that fell���four to six inches in less than two hours���resulted in some of the worst flash flooding the area has seen. Parts of Erie, Chautauqua, Cattaraugus and Allegany counties received State and Federal Disaster Declarations. In the Village of Silver Creek an entire mobile home park along Silver Creek was washed away. The flood waters continued through the heart of the village through the fire station, police headquarters and highway department. Several police cars, an ambulance and much of the town���s highway equipment were lost. In Irving, the flood waters flowed across parts of the New York State Thruway and Routes 5 and 20. Water flooded portions of Lakeshore Hospital there. Also in Irving, the Seneca Nation sustained almost two million dollars in damage to roads and bridges. In total, in Chautauqua county hundreds of homes were damaged and 45 homes were condemned, including the 35 mobile homes of the aforementioned park. In the Village of Gowanda (part of which is in Erie County and part in Cattaraugus county), extensive flash flooding occurred. Tri-County hospital in Gowanda was inundated by flood waters. Patients were transferred to Lakeshore Hospital in Irving. Several hundred homes sustained damage and 40 homes were condemned there. One fatality occurred in Gowanda as an 80-year-old man ventured out to check a bridge on his property and was swept away by the flood waters. The gage on Cattaraugus Creek at Gowanda showed a rise from six feet to near 13 feet in less than an hour (flood stage is 10 feet). Throughout Cattaraugus county over one-quarter of the roads were damaged or lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A favorable cold, unstable air flow over the relatively mild waters of Lake Ontario developed a nearly stationary fetch of lake effect snow bands from Lake Ontario into portions of northern New York during the morning of December 10th. Localized snow bands delivered 6 to 12 inches across portions of southeast St. Lawrence and southern Franklin counties in New York, including 12 inches in Wanakena, 10 inches in Star Lake, and Tupper Lake.

Primary low pressure traveled from the Great Lakes across the New York/Canadian border on January 7th. Meanwhile, a coastal low slowly developed south of New England during the late afternoon of the 7th and eventually intensified as it moved into the Gulf of Maine during the night. ||Snow overspread northern New York during the early morning hours of the 7th and continued until midnight on the 8th. Total snowfall accumulations were generally 6 to 9 inches with a brief period of sleet mixing with the snow at times.

A storm system located across the southern Plains on the morning of January 27th moved into the Ohio River Valley by the morning of January 28th. This storm moved into New York during the afternoon of the 28th and gradually weakened. Meanwhile, a coastal low formed along the southern New England coast during the afternoon of the 28th and intensified as it moved into the Gulf of Maine during the evening and into New Brunswick by midnight.||Snow overspread northern New York just before dawn and continued to accumulate into the early evening hours before tapering to scattered snow showers prior to midnight. Snowfall accumulations with this storm were generally 6 to 10 inches in northern New York. There were numerous reports of motor vehicle accidents throughout northern New York.

An upper atmospheric area of low pressure and weak surface low moved across northern New York on the morning of December 28th. Periods of light snow occurred from the pre-dawn through the early afternoon hours on December 28th which resulted in 1 to 3 inches of snow accumulation.||An arctic cold front and upper atmospheric disturbance moved across northern New York during the early morning hours of December 29th with snow showers and localized snow squalls. Widespread snowfall accumulations of 1 to 2 inches occurred with localized amounts up to 4 inches. ||Two-day storm totals were 3 to 7 inches across much of northern New York.||Rapidly falling temperatures to near zero degrees along with falling and blowing snow before and during the morning commute of December 29th accounted for roads to flash freeze that resulted in numerous vehicle accidents.

A strong and persistent east northeast wind gradient existed over the region between strong low pressure off the Carolinas (remnants of Ida) and a strong high pressure system located over Eastern Canada between November 11th and the 14th. The tidal piling over several tidal cycles caused widespread moderate coastal flooding along the south shore back bays of Queens, Brooklyn and Long Island and the eastern bays of Long Island. Minor to locally moderate coastal flooding occurred along New York Harbor and Western Long Island Sound. High seas and long period easterly swells also caused significant beach erosion along Atlantic Ocean facing beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man surfing alone in the waters off Rockaway Beach drowned when his leg strap became tangled and rough surf pulled him underwater.

There was also erosion on the east side of Field 5.  Field 5 may have to be closed next summer due to the lack of beach.||At Davis Park on Fire Island, 70 percent of the sand added to the beach in a community funded project was swept away.

An arctic cold front moved across northern New York during the late evening of January 13th which delivered some of the coldest temperatures across the region in several years. As the arctic front passed across northern New York on the night of the 13th, temperatures plummeted over 20 degrees within a few hours. Massena reported a 20 degree temperature drop within an hour...going from 30 degrees at 9 pm EST to 10 degrees at 10 pm EST.||Temperatures averaged 20 to 25 degrees below normal values, which were already at climatological winter minimums. Daytime maximum temperatures ranged from single digits above and below zero during this stretch while nighttime minimums were 10 to 30 below zero.||Massena (St. Lawrence county) experienced temperatures at or below zero for 76 consecutive hours, between 1 am EST January 14th to 5 am EST January 17th.||Some observed minimum temperatures for January 15th included: 27 degrees below zero at Massena, Norfolk (St. Lawrence county), Brasher Falls (Franklin county) and Ellenburg Depot (Clinton county) with 22 degrees below zero at Fort Covington (St. Lawrence county) and Malone (Franklin county) and 20 degrees below zero at Saranac Lake.||Some observed minimum temperatures for January 16th included: 31 degrees below in Newcomb (Essex county), 28 degrees below in Gouverneur (St. Lawrence county), 27 degrees below zero in Saranac Lake (Franklin county), 25 degrees below zero in Plattsburgh (Clinton county) and 23 degrees below zero in Massena and Edwards (St. Lawrence county).||These extremely cold temperatures led to numerous cold weather related problems including numerous dead vehicle batteries and broken water pipes.

Daytime maximum temperatures ranged from single digits above and below zero during this stretch while nighttime minimums were 10 to 30 below zero.||Massena (St. Lawrence county) experienced temperatures at or below zero for 76 consecutive hours, between 1 am EST January 14th to 5 am EST January 17th.||Some observed minimum temperatures for January 15th included: 27 degrees below zero at Massena, Norfolk (St. Lawrence county), Brasher Falls (Franklin county) and Ellenburg Depot (Clinton county) with 22 degrees below zero at Fort Covington (St. Lawrence county) and Malone (Franklin county) and 20 degrees below zero at Saranac Lake.||Some observed minimum temperatures for January 16th included: 31 degrees below in Newcomb (Essex county), 28 degrees below in Gouverneur (St. Lawrence county), 27 degrees below zero in Saranac Lake (Franklin county), 25 degrees below zero in Plattsburgh (Clinton county) and 23 degrees below zero in Massena and Edwards (St. Lawrence county).||These extremely cold temperatures led to numerous cold weather related problems including numerous dead vehicle batteries and broken water pipes.

As the arctic front passed across northern New York on the night of the 13th, temperatures plummeted over 20 degrees within a few hours. Massena reported a 20 degree temperature drop within an hour...going from 30 degrees at 9 pm EST to 10 degrees at 10 pm EST.||Temperatures averaged 20 to 25 degrees below normal values, which were already at climatological winter minimums. Daytime maximum temperatures ranged from single digits above and below zero during this stretch while nighttime minimums were 10 to 30 below zero.||Massena (St. Lawrence county) experienced temperatures at or below zero for 76 consecutive hours, between 1 am EST January 14th to 5 am EST January 17th.||Some observed minimum temperatures for January 15th included: 27 degrees below zero at Massena, Norfolk (St. Lawrence county), Brasher Falls (Franklin county) and Ellenburg Depot (Clinton county) with 22 degrees below zero at Fort Covington (St. Lawrence county) and Malone (Franklin county) and 20 degrees below zero at Saranac Lake.||Some observed minimum temperatures for January 16th included: 31 degrees below in Newcomb (Essex county), 28 degrees below in Gouverneur (St. Lawrence county), 27 degrees below zero in Saranac Lake (Franklin county), 25 degrees below zero in Plattsburgh (Clinton county) and 23 degrees below zero in Massena and Edwards (St. Lawrence county).||These extremely cold temperatures led to numerous cold weather related problems including numerous dead vehicle batteries and broken water pipes.

A strong and persistent east northeast wind gradient existed over the region between strong low pressure off the Carolinas (remnants of Ida) and a strong high pressure system located over Eastern Canada between November 11th and the 14th. The tidal piling over several tidal cycles caused widespread moderate coastal flooding along the south shore back bays of Queens, Brooklyn and Long Island and the eastern bays of Long Island. Minor to locally moderate coastal flooding occurred along New York Harbor and Western Long Island Sound. High seas and long period easterly swells also caused significant beach erosion along Atlantic Ocean facing beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water rose to 5.40 ft. (NGVD) at 612 am (EST) at the USGS gauge in Hudson Bay at Freeport.  This is .40 feet above the moderate coastal flooding benchmark of 5.0 ft.||Water rose to 5.61 ft. (NGVD) at the USGS gauge in East Rockaway Inlet at Atlantic Beach.  This is .11 feet above the moderate coastal flooding benchmark of 5.5 ft.||Water rose to 5.50 ft. (NGVD) at the USGS gauge in Point Lookout at Reynolds Channel.  This is at the moderate coastal flooding benchmark of 5.5 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper atmospheric area of low pressure and weak surface low moved across northern New York on the morning of December 28th. Periods of light snow occurred from the pre-dawn through the early afternoon hours on December 28th which resulted in 1 to 3 inches of snow accumulation.||An arctic cold front and upper atmospheric disturbance moved across northern New York during the early morning hours of December 29th with snow showers and localized snow squalls. Widespread snowfall accumulations of 1 to 2 inches occurred with localized amounts up to 4 inches. ||Two-day storm totals were 3 to 7 inches across much of northern New York.||Rapidly falling temperatures to near zero degrees along with falling and blowing snow before and during the morning commute of December 29th accounted for roads to flash freeze that resulted in numerous vehicle accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed between a deep low pressure system northeast of Maine and a strong high pressure system over the Southeast United States.  Peak wind gusts ranged from 40 mph to 55 mph.  They downed tree limbs and caused scattered power outages across Southeast NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 44 mph was measured by a mesonet station in Huntington Station.  Law enforcement reported downed tree limbs and trees in Fort Salonga and Stony Brook.  A tree fell on and damaged a pickup truck in Stony Brook.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with heavy rain downed numerous trees and power lines across Orange County causing around a thousand power outages.

Moist, unstable air ahead of an approaching cold front set off scattered strong to severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening. The Lake Erie breeze served as focal point for initial severe convection that produced quarter sized hail over Wyoming County. Over the eastern Lake Ontario region, the thunderstorms downed trees and power lines in Jefferson County. In Philadelphia, a 14��� x 80��� trailer was flipped by the downburst winds. As the thunderstorms moved across southern Erie County, the heavy rain (over four inches in some locations) produced flash flooding that damaged road shoulders���including Route 62, Schintzius and Jennings Roads and Larkin Street.

A massive convective complex moved from lower Michigan across southern Ontario near Toronto then dove southeast across the Niagara Frontier and Western Southern Tier. Damage reports were fairly widespread across the area with downed power lines, limbs and trees. Power outages resulted from the downed power lines. Several homes and automobiles were damaged by felled trees. The heavy rains that accompanied the thunderstorms produced some flash flooding in Chautauqua County. Small creeks overflowed and parking lots were flooded in Jamestown. In Lakewood, numerous roads and viaducts were flooded and closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably deep area of low pressure over the upper Great Lakes swept a powerful cold front across the Niagara Frontier and western Finger Lakes Region. The thunderstorms which accompanied the cold front produced strong, damaging winds. Gusts were measured to 61 mph. The winds downed trees and power lines throughout the region with utilities reporting tens of thousands of customers without service.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforecment reported trees and debris on Route 33.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked across Ontario and Quebec dragging a cold front across the region. Thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines in West Sparta, Livingston County.|Another thunderstorm accompanying the front produced a tornado in Ontario County. The tornado initially touched down in the Town of Bristol then moved to the City of Canandaigua.  Along West Ridge Run several homes suffered damage to soffits and siding. There was also some structural damage of the homes due to trees falling onto the structures. The tornado produced damage along the west shore of Canandaigua Lake at the Canandaigua Yacht Club. Widespread tree damage was observed at that location with several trees snapped off well above ground. Numerous boats were heavily damaged including a 6500 pound boat, housed in a hoist along a dock that was thrown about 40 yards into the lake where it sunk. Several houses along the lakeshore in the vicinity of Lakeview Lane also sustained minor damage to siding and roofs as well as broken windows from falling trees. The tornado finally lifted in the vicinity of Smith Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed by thunderstorm winds.

A massive convective complex moved from lower Michigan across southern Ontario near Toronto then dove southeast across the Niagara Frontier and Western Southern Tier. This followed an earlier round of strong thunderstorms and heavy rains earlier in the day. Damage from the thunderstorm winds was widespread across the Niagara Frontier and Western Southern Tier.  Trees as large as two to three feet in diameter were downed. Power outages were scattered throughout the region as the winds downed power lines.|The unprecedented heavy rains that fell���four to six inches in less than two hours���resulted in some of the worst flash flooding the area has seen. Parts of Erie, Chautauqua, Cattaraugus and Allegany counties received State and Federal Disaster Declarations. In the Village of Silver Creek an entire mobile home park along Silver Creek was washed away. The flood waters continued through the heart of the village through the fire station, police headquarters and highway department. Several police cars, an ambulance and much of the town���s highway equipment were lost. In Irving, the flood waters flowed across parts of the New York State Thruway and Routes 5 and 20. Water flooded portions of Lakeshore Hospital there. Also in Irving, the Seneca Nation sustained almost two million dollars in damage to roads and bridges. In total, in Chautauqua county hundreds of homes were damaged and 45 homes were condemned, including the 35 mobile homes of the aforementioned park. In the Village of Gowanda (part of which is in Erie County and part in Cattaraugus county), extensive flash flooding occurred. Tri-County hospital in Gowanda was inundated by flood waters. Patients were transferred to Lakeshore Hospital in Irving. Several hundred homes sustained damage and 40 homes were condemned there. One fatality occurred in Gowanda as an 80-year-old man ventured out to check a bridge on his property and was swept away by the flood waters. The gage on Cattaraugus Creek at Gowanda showed a rise from six feet to near 13 feet in less than an hour (flood stage is 10 feet). Throughout Cattaraugus county over one-quarter of the roads were damaged or lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed by thunderstorm winds.

Scattered thunderstorms quickly developed along lake breeze boundaries during the mid afternoon in a strong unstable environment. An isolated storm over Wayne County produced three-quarter inch hail in Newark. Another storm over Niagara County produced damaging winds that downed tree limbs and wires in Wheatfield. The heavy rain that accompanied the storms fell on already saturated ground and produced flash flooding in Jamestown, Lockport were viaducts and roads were flooded and near Clyde where roads were damaged near the intersection of State Route 414 and Kelsey Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked across Ontario and Quebec dragging a cold front across the region. Thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines in West Sparta, Livingston County.|Another thunderstorm accompanying the front produced a tornado in Ontario County. The tornado initially touched down in the Town of Bristol then moved to the City of Canandaigua.  Along West Ridge Run several homes suffered damage to soffits and siding. There was also some structural damage of the homes due to trees falling onto the structures. The tornado produced damage along the west shore of Canandaigua Lake at the Canandaigua Yacht Club. Widespread tree damage was observed at that location with several trees snapped off well above ground. Numerous boats were heavily damaged including a 6500 pound boat, housed in a hoist along a dock that was thrown about 40 yards into the lake where it sunk. Several houses along the lakeshore in the vicinity of Lakeview Lane also sustained minor damage to siding and roofs as well as broken windows from falling trees. The tornado finally lifted in the vicinity of Smith Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey confirmed tornado touchdown.

Several police cars, an ambulance and much of the town���s highway equipment were lost. In Irving, the flood waters flowed across parts of the New York State Thruway and Routes 5 and 20. Water flooded portions of Lakeshore Hospital there. Also in Irving, the Seneca Nation sustained almost two million dollars in damage to roads and bridges. In total, in Chautauqua county hundreds of homes were damaged and 45 homes were condemned, including the 35 mobile homes of the aforementioned park. In the Village of Gowanda (part of which is in Erie County and part in Cattaraugus county), extensive flash flooding occurred. Tri-County hospital in Gowanda was inundated by flood waters. Patients were transferred to Lakeshore Hospital in Irving. Several hundred homes sustained damage and 40 homes were condemned there. One fatality occurred in Gowanda as an 80-year-old man ventured out to check a bridge on his property and was swept away by the flood waters. The gage on Cattaraugus Creek at Gowanda showed a rise from six feet to near 13 feet in less than an hour (flood stage is 10 feet). Throughout Cattaraugus county over one-quarter of the roads were damaged or lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous storms developed across the Western Southern Tier during the early afternoon, then spread and developed east northeast into the Finger Lakes and eventually the Tug Hill region. Isolated severe storms developed in Cattaraugus and Wayne counties where trees and wires were downed in Ischua and Ontario.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system strengthened as it moved northeastward along the east coast Friday, November 27th. The low passed south and east of Cape Cod Friday morning into the afternoon hours and deepened further as it headed to the Gulf of Maine Friday afternoon and night. The low continued to deepen as it moved to the Canadian Maritimes Saturday.||Generally, wind gusts were between 35 and 55 mph with a few higher gusts around 60 mph. The strong and gusty winds resulted in scattered power outages and downed trees and tree limbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severel trees were reported down due to high winds in West Hurley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate tidal flooding was caused by a strong pressure gradient between high pressure to the north and a coastal storm passing south of Long Island October 17th and 18th.||A prolonged period of strong northeast winds across coastal waters coupled with astronomically high tides caused water to build along the coast followed by tidal piling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streets were flooded in Lindenhurst around the time of high tide.||Beach Erosion was reported at Robert Moses State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the passage of a strong cold front, strong west winds developed.  Peak gusts ranged from around 45 mph to 55 mph and caused scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts of 56 mph was measured by ASOS at JFK Airport at 12:18 pm (EDT).||Peak wind gusts of 50 mph was measured by ASOS at LaGuardia Airport at 12:22 pm (EDT).||Tree limbs fell on a car, through a rear window and caved in the back of the roof in Queens.||In Howard Beach, a tree landed on a car on 157th Avenue and 83rd Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate tidal flooding was caused by a strong pressure gradient between high pressure to the north and a coastal storm passing south of Long Island Thursday night October 15th and Friday, October 16th. ||A prolonged period of strong east to northeast winds across coastal waters coupled with astronomically high tides caused water to build along the coast followed by tidal piling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported in Freeport at 5:17 am (EDT), on Bayville Avenue at 10:45 (EDT) am in Bayville, and on Sheep Lane in Lattingtown at 10:45 am (EDT), with a gauge measurment reported by the Nassau County OEM of 7.35 feet.  Several cars were inundated with water at the intersection of Brookville Blvd and Rockaway Blvd at 7 am (EDT).  The USGS gauge at Freeport exceeded its moderate flood stage of 5.8 feet MLLW, peaking at 6.11 feet during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate tidal flooding was caused by a strong pressure gradient between high pressure to the north and a coastal storm passing south of Long Island October 17th and 18th.||A prolonged period of strong northeast winds across coastal waters coupled with astronomically high tides caused water to build along the coast followed by tidal piling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water rose to 7.17 feet MLLW at the USGS tidal gauge in Atlantic Beach at 8:30 am (EDT).  This is .17 feet above its bench mark for moderate tidal flooding.|Water rose to 6.78 feet MLLW at the USGS tidal gauge in Point Lookout at 8:54 am (EDT).  This is .18 feet above its bench mark for moderate tidal flooding.|Roads near the intersection of Woodcliff Ave and Front Street were reported inundated and impassable and water was reported up to the doors of commercial buildings in Freeport at 8:45 am (EDT)|At 8:56 am (EDT) a maximum water level of 6.32 feet MLLW was reported at the USGS gauge in Freeport, this is 0.52 feet above the bench mark for moderate tidal flooding. Also at this time in Freeport, a few feet of water was reported over Hudson Street, and several streets along the Nautical Mile were under water and impassable.|At 9:00 am (EDT) a maximum water level of 8.96 feet MLLW was reported at the USGS gauge at Inwood, this is 0.26 feet above the bench mark for moderate tidal flooding.|At 11:50 am (EDT) overwash flooding was reported on Bayville Avenue in Bayview.|At 12:00 pm (EDT) a maximum water level of 10.51 feet MLLW was reported at the NOS gauge at Kings Point, this is 0.01 feet above the bench mark for moderate tidal flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coastal flooding was reported at the intersection of Brookville Blvd and Rockaway Blvd, with one car stranded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal low pressure rapidly intensified Saturday night as it tracked northeast from off the Delmarva Coast...just southeast of the 40N/70W benchmark���and to southeast of Cape Cod by Sunday morning. This was in response to a powerful upper low approaching and then crossing south of the area. The track of low pressure to the southeast of the region and stubborn placement of arctic high pressure over the Northern US Plains and Southern Canadian Plains...ridging eastward...made for the ideal setup for heavy snow across most of the region. The rapid intensification of low pressure created blizzard or near blizzard conditions over Long Island for several hours with northeast winds of 15 to 25 mph and gusts 35 to 45 mph���and visibilities below �� mile. Near whiteout conds were experienced across Suffolk County during the height of the storm. Snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches per hour occurred with an intense mesoscale band that developed S of the region���pivoted northwest into West/Central Long Island���and then slowly pulled east through the overnight. Snow totals increased rapidly from northwest to southeast across the region...with 3 to 6 inches across Orange County���around 1 ft across the NYC metro���and around 2 ft across Suffolk County.

A strong cold front produced thunderstorms over Long Island and the adjacent coastal waters. Strong winds were produced by these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported that trees and wires were downed by strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms developed in a tropical airmass, which resulted in very heavy rain and flash flooding in Orange County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All lanes were closed northbound and southbound on NY 218 between Cornwall-on-Hudson and West Point due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms producing heavy rainfall moved across Orange County during the late evening hours which resulted in isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 17 in Goshen was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system associated with low pressure moving through the Great Lakes region, produced rainfall over a period of 18 to 24 hours which caused isolated flooding on Staten Island and in Westchester County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 9 was closed at Hudson Ave. due to flooding.  The Saw Mill River Pkwy was also closed both northbound and southbound at exit 29 (Pleasantville Rd.).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system associated with low pressure moving through the Great Lakes region, produced rainfall over a period of 18 to 24 hours which caused isolated flooding on Staten Island and in Westchester County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several motorists were stranded in high water (approximately 2 ft. deep) on Page Ave. near Amboy Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the passage of a strong cold front, strong west winds developed.  Peak gusts ranged from around 45 mph to 55 mph and caused scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds downed trees and tree limbs in parts of the Williamsburg and Gravesend sections of Brooklyn.  In Bensonhurst, a tree fell on a house on 21st Avenue that caused structural damage.  Parts of Dyker Heights reported power failures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front transiting the Tri-State triggered multiple severe thunderstorms across Southeastern New York, especially across northern portions of the Lower Hudson Valley and Eastern Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six trees were reported snapped in the backyard at 113 Curbytown Road in Middletown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front transiting the Tri-State triggered multiple severe thunderstorms across Southeastern New York, especially across northern portions of the Lower Hudson Valley and Eastern Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches were reported down in Mahopac.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front transiting the Tri-State triggered multiple severe thunderstorms across Southeastern New York, especially across northern portions of the Lower Hudson Valley and Eastern Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported blocking a road in the Sound Cliffs area East of Mattituck Inlet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the passage of a strong cold front, strong west winds developed.  Peak gusts ranged from around 45 mph to 55 mph and caused scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds downed trees and tree limbs onto wires and cars.  Power failures were reported in the Huguenot and Huguenot Beach sections.  A tree fell on the Staten Island Railway's St. George-bound train track near Grasmere station around 1:40 pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man drowned while swimming off Fire Island, Near Cherry Grove, the afternoon of the 18th.  Suffolk police state the man was carried away from shore by a strong current.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 22 year old man died while swimming off Fire Island, near Cherry Grove, around 5 pm. Suffolk Police state the man was pulled away from shore by a strong current.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of high pressure to the northeast and a developing coastal low to the south produced  strong northeast winds over the region, piling water up in the Back Bays of Long Island.  Coastal flooding was measured at Freeport and observed at Lindenhurst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 1.5 feet of water covered East Shore Road, and East Riviera Drive from the Great South Bay to Near East Neptune Avenue (about 2 and one half blocks from the Bay).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated pulse severe thunderstorm moved along the sea breeze front across Northern Nassau County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tree limbs and power lines were reported down throughout the town of Glenn Cove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated pulse severe thunderstorm moved along the sea breeze front across Northern Nassau County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down in Oyster Bay Cove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a passing pre-frontal trough and an approaching cold front spawned multiple severe thunderstorms across the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down in Warwick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a passing pre-frontal trough and an approaching cold front spawned multiple severe thunderstorms across the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on New York State Highway 94 north of Warwick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a passing pre-frontal trough and an approaching cold front spawned multiple severe thunderstorms across the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down in Stony Point, including some into houses. In addition, numerous wires were reported down as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a passing pre-frontal trough and an approaching cold front spawned multiple severe thunderstorms across the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large tree limbs were reported down on Gardnertown Road near Route 52.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a passing pre-frontal trough and an approaching cold front spawned multiple severe thunderstorms across the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on New York Route 129 on the East side of Hunter Brook Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system strengthened as it moved northeastward along the east coast Friday, November 27th. The low passed south and east of Cape Cod Friday morning into the afternoon hours and deepened further as it headed to the Gulf of Maine Friday afternoon and night. The low continued to deepen as it moved to the Canadian Maritimes Saturday.||Generally, wind gusts were between 35 and 55 mph with a few higher gusts around 60 mph. The strong and gusty winds resulted in scattered power outages and downed trees and tree limbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power outages were reported due to high winds in western Ulster County, particularly in Woodstock, Wawarsing and Olive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across northern New York during the late evening of January 13th which delivered some of the coldest temperatures across the region in several years. As the arctic front passed across northern New York on the night of the 13th, temperatures plummeted over 20 degrees within a few hours. Massena reported a 20 degree temperature drop within an hour...going from 30 degrees at 9 pm EST to 10 degrees at 10 pm EST.||Temperatures averaged 20 to 25 degrees below normal values, which were already at climatological winter minimums. Daytime maximum temperatures ranged from single digits above and below zero during this stretch while nighttime minimums were 10 to 30 below zero.||Massena (St. Lawrence county) experienced temperatures at or below zero for 76 consecutive hours, between 1 am EST January 14th to 5 am EST January 17th.||Some observed minimum temperatures for January 15th included: 27 degrees below zero at Massena, Norfolk (St. Lawrence county), Brasher Falls (Franklin county) and Ellenburg Depot (Clinton county) with 22 degrees below zero at Fort Covington (St. Lawrence county) and Malone (Franklin county) and 20 degrees below zero at Saranac Lake.||Some observed minimum temperatures for January 16th included: 31 degrees below in Newcomb (Essex county), 28 degrees below in Gouverneur (St. Lawrence county), 27 degrees below zero in Saranac Lake (Franklin county), 25 degrees below zero in Plattsburgh (Clinton county) and 23 degrees below zero in Massena and Edwards (St. Lawrence county).||These extremely cold temperatures led to numerous cold weather related problems including numerous dead vehicle batteries and broken water pipes.

The strong and gusty winds resulted in scattered power outages and downed trees and tree limbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power outages were reported due to high winds across northern Warren County, particularly in Johnsburg and Stony Creek.

A low pressure system and an upper level disturbance moved over the region Monday, December 28th, resulting in a light to moderate snowfall. A tight pressure gradient developed Tuesday morning, December 29th, as the low strengthened as it headed northeast and high pressure built in from the west. Strong northwest winds with gusts of 30 to 50 mph led to blowing and drifting of the snow.

A low pressure system and an upper level disturbance moved over the region Monday, December 28th, resulting in a light to moderate snowfall. A tight pressure gradient developed Tuesday morning, December 29th, as the low strengthened as it headed northeast and high pressure built in from the west. Strong northwest winds with gusts of 30 to 50 mph led to blowing and drifting of the snow.

A large and powerful low pressure system over the upper midwest moved slowly northeastward as it weakened on Saturday, December 26th. In the meantime, a secondary low had formed over the mid atlantic region and moved gradually northeast. A light wintry mix of precipitation overspread east central New York Christmas night and continued into Saturday night, causing travel to be treacherous at times.  ||Snow and sleet accumulations of 1 to 3 inches occurred, along with ice accretions of one to two tenths of an inch.

A strong low pressure system tracked northeast, into the eastern Great Lakes region during Wednesday, December 9th. As the pressure gradient tightened between this approaching low pressure system, and retreating high pressure across southeast Canada, strong east to southeast winds developed across portions of the central Taconics in eastern New York during Wednesday morning, before gradually diminishing by Wednesday evening. ||Local topographical features enhanced the winds, especially across central and eastern portions of Rensselaer County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A radio tower was ripped from its foundation and a half mile swath of trees were knocked down or snapped in half on Dunham Hollow Road in Averill Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly flow of cold air moved across the relatively warmer waters of Lake Ontario resulting in the development of bands of snow which extended across northern Herkimer County into Hamilton County. Disturbances in the upper levels of the atmosphere moved over the region and helped to produce very heavy snow with snowfall rates as high as 2 to 4 inches an hour which led to near whiteout conditions at times. ||The heaviest snow occurred along Route 28 from around Old Forge in Herkimer County to around Inlet in Hamilton County, where snowfall amounts of 12 to 15 inches were reported.

Deep low pressure strengthened as it moved from near Chicago to Quebec. The strong southerly winds ahead of the system downed trees and power lines across the higher elevations of the western southern Tier of New York during the pre-dawn hours. On the New York State Thruway, several tractor trailers were blown over as winds gusted to near 70 mph.  As the system passed to the north, winds shifted to southwest and increased.  Gusts were measured to 60 mph at the Buffalo International Airport. The winds downed trees and power lines and utilities reported tens of thousands without power.

A low pressure system originating over Texas moved northeast and across the central and eastern Great Lakes Wednesday, December 9th. A secondary low formed over the mid Atlantic region early Wednesday morning and strengthened as it moved northeast across Long Island and southern New England during the day. ||Snow overspread east central New York between 3 AM and 5 AM LST Wednesday morning. The snow was heavy at times with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches an hour |in some locations. Some warmer air worked in aloft causing sleet to mix with the snow during the day Wednesday.  The precipitation became light and tapered off by early Wednesday afternoon as it changed to rain below 1000 feet, and freezing rain above 1000 feet in elevation. Snowfall totals ranged from around 5 inches up to 12 inches.

Gusts were measured to 60 mph at the Buffalo International Airport. The winds downed trees and power lines and utilities reported tens of thousands without power.

A low pressure system originating over Texas moved northeast and across the central and eastern Great Lakes Wednesday, December 9th. A secondary low formed over the mid Atlantic region early Wednesday morning and strengthened as it moved northeast across Long Island and southern New England during the day. ||Snow overspread east central New York between 3 AM and 5 AM LST Wednesday morning. The snow was heavy at times with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches an hour |in some locations. Some warmer air worked in aloft causing sleet to mix with the snow during the day Wednesday.  The precipitation became light and tapered off by early Wednesday afternoon as it changed to rain below 1000 feet, and freezing rain above 1000 feet in elevation. Snowfall totals ranged from around 5 inches up to 12 inches.

A strong westerly flow of cold air moved across the relatively warmer waters of Lake Ontario resulting in the development of bands of snow which extended across northern Herkimer County into Hamilton County. Disturbances in the upper levels of the atmosphere moved over the region and helped to produce very heavy snow with snowfall rates as high as 2 to 4 inches an hour which led to near whiteout conditions at times. ||The heaviest snow occurred along Route 28 from around Old Forge in Herkimer County to around Inlet in Hamilton County, where snowfall amounts of 12 to 15 inches were reported.

A large and powerful low pressure system over the upper midwest moved slowly northeastward as it weakened on Saturday, December 26th. In the meantime, a secondary low had formed over the mid atlantic region and moved gradually northeast. A light wintry mix of precipitation overspread east central New York Christmas night and continued into Saturday night, causing travel to be treacherous at times.  ||Snow and sleet accumulations of 1 to 3 inches occurred, along with ice accretions of one to two tenths of an inch.

A low pressure system and an upper level disturbance moved over the region Monday, December 28th, resulting in a light to moderate snowfall. A tight pressure gradient developed Tuesday morning, December 29th, as the low strengthened as it headed northeast and high pressure built in from the west. Strong northwest winds with gusts of 30 to 50 mph led to blowing and drifting of the snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations of up to four tenths of an inch along with brisk winds resulted in widespread power outages across southern Herkimer County, mainly in areas above 1000 feet. The majority of the outages occurred in Dolgeville, however the towns of Manheim, Salisbury and Frankfort were also affected.

A low pressure system tracked northeast from the Tennessee Valley, across Pennsylvania, to coastal New England. This system brought mainly snow to central New York, with a mix of precipitation falling across the Catskills and northeast Pennsylvania. Between 5 and 10 inches of snow fell across north central New York State.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought snowfall amounts of 6 to 9 inches to the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow developed and persisted over parts of the Catskills in the wake of a departing low pressure system that intensified off the east coast. Snowfall rates were around 1 inch per hour or greater in the most intense part of the band. Snowfall totals of between 5 and 10 inches were reported in most areas with a few locations seeing over a foot.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy band of snow brought snowfall amounts of 5 to 10 inches to parts of the county in just a few hours time. Hancock measured 10.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the mid Mississippi Valley to the Great Lakes region from the evening of the 8th to the afternoon of the 9th. This storm spread snow northeast across central New York during the early morning of the 9th. The snow tapered to patchy freezing rain and rain by late morning of the 9th before ending. The heaviest snowfall was in Sullivan County where an average of 7 inches fell. Across the rest of central New York and northeast Pennsylvania, 3 to 6 inches of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of 7 inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense lake effect snow band dropped south into the far northern part of Oneida County late Friday afternoon on the 11th, and continued until the early morning hours of the 12th. Storm total snowfall ranged from 8 to 17 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall range from 8 to 17 inches with the highest amounts recorded near Boonville.

Coastal low pressure rapidly intensified Saturday night as it tracked northeast from off the Delmarva Coast, just southeast of the 40N/70W benchmark, and to southeast of Cape Cod by Sunday morning.  Gale force north to northeast winds developed high surf which pounded the shoreline through the weekend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten feet of dunes on the east side of Field 5 at Robert Moses State Park were carved away by high surf.

A light wintry mix of precipitation overspread east central New York Christmas night and continued into Saturday night, causing travel to be treacherous at times.  ||Snow and sleet accumulations of 1 to 3 inches occurred, along with ice accretions of one to two tenths of an inch.

A deep upper low tracking northeast through the Central United States was responsible for a rapidly intensifying low pressure system tracking into the Great Lakes.  A strong upper level jet rounding up the East Coast induced secondary low pressure along an associated warm front off the Mid-Atlantic Coast. This low tracked northeast along the coast...producing a 6 to 8 hour period of heavy precipitation across the region. A marginally cold thermal profile and strong dynamics allowed for a 2 to 4 hour period of moderate to heavy snow across interior zones...with snowfall rates up to 1 inch per hour. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6.0 inches at Newburgh to 7.0 inches at Westtown, including 6.2 inches at Middletown.
A strong pressure gradient between strong low pressure to the north and high pressure building from the west resulted in strong northwest winds across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust to 61 mph was reported at Great Gull Island at 1053 am. These strong winds knocked down a tree in North Babylon. The tree took down a high tension wire which ignited a pickup truck. The truck fire occured on Wickfield Lane around 855 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between strong low pressure to the north and high pressure building from the west resulted in strong northwest winds across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 63 mph was measured in Bayville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of gusty winds, and low temperatures during the early morning hours of Thursday January 1st produced wind chills of -15 to |-20 F across the higher elevations of the eastern Catskills and southern Taconics, and -20 to -25 F across the southern Adirondacks, western Mohawk Valley, northern and central Taconics, the Schoharie Valley and Helderbergs, and across the Lake George region.

The combination of gusty winds, and low temperatures during the early morning hours of Thursday January 1st produced wind chills of -15 to |-20 F across the higher elevations of the eastern Catskills and southern Taconics, and -20 to -25 F across the southern Adirondacks, western Mohawk Valley, northern and central Taconics, the Schoharie Valley and Helderbergs, and across the Lake George region.

A low pressure system tracked from the lower Ohio Valley on Tuesday January 6th, into the eastern Great Lakes during Wednesday January 7th, and into northern New England by Thursday morning January 8th. A second low pressure system developed near Long Island during Wednesday, and tracked northeast along the northern New England coast by Thursday morning.||This combination of low pressure systems spread a swath of moderate to heavy precipitation across eastern New York and western New England, beginning late Tuesday evening, and ending early Thursday morning.||The precipitation fell as a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain across the region, before gradually changing to plain rain from south to north during Wednesday as milder air approached from the south. As colder air wrapped back into the region, the precipitation changed to snow before ending later Wednesday night and early Thursday morning.||Snow and sleet amounts ranged from 1 to 4 inches for areas south of the Mohawk River Valley, including within the Capital District, Schoharie Valley, the mid Hudson Valley and eastern Catskills, and the central and southern Taconics, with generally 4 to 8 inches further north, including within the central and eastern Mohawk Valley, and the Lake George and Saratoga region. Locally higher amounts, with 8 to 10 inches, fell across portions of the southern Adirondacks.||In addition, ice accretion from freezing rain generally ranged from one tenth, to three tenths of an inch, with the greatest amounts occurring within the immediate Capital Region north into the eastern Mohawk Valley.||This wintry mix led to widespread hazardous travel conditions during Wednesday morning. In addition, numerous closures, or delayed openings of schools and businesses occurred Wednesday morning.
