A developing low pressure system tracked northeast from the lower Ohio Valley on Saturday January 10th, into central Pennsylvania Saturday night, and then east to a position off the New England coast by Sunday morning, January 11th.||This low pressure system spread moderate to locally heavy snowfall across much of east central New York and western New England, beginning early Saturday evening, and ending Sunday morning.||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches, although locally higher amounts of between 8 and 11 inches fell across portions of the Capital Region, the eastern Catskills, the Schoharie Valley and Helderbergs.

A low pressure system developed over the lower Mississippi Valley during Tuesday January 27th. The low then strengthened, and tracked northeast from the lower Ohio Valley Wednesday morning on January 28th, into Pennsylvania during Wednesday afternoon, and into northern New England by Thursday morning January 29th.||This storm spread a significant wintry mix of precipitation across eastern New York State, with heavy snow and sleet across much of the southern Adirondacks into the Lake George Saratoga region, with a significant mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain occurring elsewhere.||Snow and sleet amounts ranged from 8 to 12 inches across the southern Adirondacks and the Lake George Saratoga region, with 4 to 8 inches occurring further south across the Mohawk River Valley, the Greater Capital District, the eastern Catskills, the Schoharie Valley, the mid Hudson Valley and into the central and southern Taconics. In addition, ice accretion from freezing rain of between one and three tenths of an inch occurred across areas near and south of the Mohawk River Valley, with locally higher amounts of up to one half of an inch occurring across portions of the mid Hudson Valley.||This wintry mix resulted in the closure of numerous schools and businesses across east central New York for both Wednesday and Thursday mornings, and also created treacherous travel conditions.

A low pressure system tracked east from the eastern Great Lakes Sunday morning January 18th, across northern New England Sunday afternoon and evening, to a position off the northern New England coast by Monday morning January 19th.||An area of light to moderate snowfall accompanied the passage of this system across much of east central New York and adjacent western New England from Sunday morning, into early Monday morning.||A narrow swath of heavier snow fell across portions of the Saratoga region, where 8 to 10 of snow accumulated. Elsewhere, snowfall amounts generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with lighter amounts falling within the immediate Capital District.||The steadiest snowfall occurred during Sunday morning, before tapering to snow showers and flurries Sunday afternoon and evening. However, another period of light snow produced an additional 1 to 2 inches of snowfall across portions of the region Sunday night. The snow ended by Monday morning.

A cold west to northwest flow of air traversing the Great Lakes, combined with the passage of an upper level disturbance, led to numerous snow showers and embedded heavier snow squalls across portions of the southwest Adirondacks, the central and eastern Mohawk Valley, the Schoharie Valley, the Saratoga region and northern portions of the Capital region during Thursday January 8th.||Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with some areas experiencing snowfall rates of between one and two inches per hour. Arctic air following a frontal passage set off lake effect snows early on Friday 16th. Initially, the activity off Lake Erie focused on the Chautauqua ridge on a west wind, with several inches Friday morning, but winds backed slightly during the midday and afternoon, and a decent lake plume lifted well to the north into Buffalo's South Towns and even up into central Erie county. Lake Erie had a large amount of ice but the snow apparently formed off some open water north of Long Point. Over 6 fell in this narrow strip with plenty of blowing snow and near whiteout conditions just to the south of the city which snarled the evening rush hour. The activity weakened during the evening and lifted north across the Buffalo metro area and dropped a quick 1-3 inches early Saturday before dissipating. Reported snowfalls included: 12 inches at Perrsyburg; 10 inches at Elma, 9 inches in East Aurora; and 7 inches at Alden.|Off Lake Ontario, the activity was stronger as ice was not a factor. Bands developed early Friday over northern Cayuga and southwest Oswego counties and gradually meandered northward during Friday and Friday night, with several intense bands setting up across the area from Pulaski to Watertown. This continued through much of Saturday before weakening during the afternoon in the Watertown area, but not before nearly two feet fell. Snowfalls totals included: 29 inches at Pulaski; 21 inches at Watertown; and 12 inches in Carthage and Oswego.

In addition, ice accretion from freezing rain of between one and three tenths of an inch occurred across areas near and south of the Mohawk River Valley, with locally higher amounts of up to one half of an inch occurring across portions of the mid Hudson Valley.||This wintry mix resulted in the closure of numerous schools and businesses across east central New York for both Wednesday and Thursday mornings, and also created treacherous travel conditions.

A developing low pressure system tracked northeast from the lower Ohio Valley on Saturday January 10th, into central Pennsylvania Saturday night, and then east to a position off the New England coast by Sunday morning, January 11th.||This low pressure system spread moderate to locally heavy snowfall across much of east central New York and western New England, beginning early Saturday evening, and ending Sunday morning.||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches, although locally higher amounts of between 8 and 11 inches fell across portions of the Capital Region, the eastern Catskills, the Schoharie Valley and Helderbergs.

A low pressure system tracked from the lower Ohio Valley on Tuesday January 6th, into the eastern Great Lakes during Wednesday January 7th, and into northern New England by Thursday morning January 8th. A second low pressure system developed near Long Island during Wednesday, and tracked northeast along the northern New England coast by Thursday morning.||This combination of low pressure systems spread a swath of moderate to heavy precipitation across eastern New York and western New England, beginning late Tuesday evening, and ending early Thursday morning.||The precipitation fell as a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain across the region, before gradually changing to plain rain from south to north during Wednesday as milder air approached from the south. Locally higher amounts, with 8 to 10 inches, fell across portions of the southern Adirondacks.||In addition, ice accretion from freezing rain generally ranged from one tenth, to three tenths of an inch, with the greatest amounts occurring within the immediate Capital Region north into the eastern Mohawk Valley.||This wintry mix led to widespread hazardous travel conditions during Wednesday morning. In addition, numerous closures, or delayed openings of schools and businesses occurred Wednesday morning.

A developing low pressure system tracked northeast from the lower Ohio Valley on Saturday January 10th, into central Pennsylvania Saturday night, and then east to a position off the New England coast by Sunday morning, January 11th.||This low pressure system spread moderate to locally heavy snowfall across much of east central New York and western New England, beginning early Saturday evening, and ending Sunday morning.||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches, although locally higher amounts of between 8 and 11 inches fell across portions of the Capital Region, the eastern Catskills, the Schoharie Valley and Helderbergs.

A low pressure system tracked from the lower Ohio Valley on Tuesday January 6th, into the eastern Great Lakes during Wednesday January 7th, and into northern New England by Thursday morning January 8th. A second low pressure system developed near Long Island during Wednesday, and tracked northeast along the northern New England coast by Thursday morning.||This combination of low pressure systems spread a swath of moderate to heavy precipitation across eastern New York and western New England, beginning late Tuesday evening, and ending early Thursday morning.||The precipitation fell as a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain across the region, before gradually changing to plain rain from south to north during Wednesday as milder air approached from the south. As colder air wrapped back into the region, the precipitation changed to snow before ending later Wednesday night and early Thursday morning.||Snow and sleet amounts ranged from 1 to 4 inches for areas south of the Mohawk River Valley, including within the Capital District, Schoharie Valley, the mid Hudson Valley and eastern Catskills, and the central and southern Taconics, with generally 4 to 8 inches further north, including within the central and eastern Mohawk Valley, and the Lake George and Saratoga region. Locally higher amounts, with 8 to 10 inches, fell across portions of the southern Adirondacks.||In addition, ice accretion from freezing rain generally ranged from one tenth, to three tenths of an inch, with the greatest amounts occurring within the immediate Capital Region north into the eastern Mohawk Valley.||This wintry mix led to widespread hazardous travel conditions during Wednesday morning. In addition, numerous closures, or delayed openings of schools and businesses occurred Wednesday morning.

A low pressure system tracked east from the eastern Great Lakes Sunday morning January 18th, across northern New England Sunday afternoon and evening, to a position off the northern New England coast by Monday morning January 19th.||An area of light to moderate snowfall accompanied the passage of this system across much of east central New York and adjacent western New England from Sunday morning, into early Monday morning.||A narrow swath of heavier snow fell across portions of the Saratoga region, where 8 to 10 of snow accumulated. Elsewhere, snowfall amounts generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with lighter amounts falling within the immediate Capital District.||The steadiest snowfall occurred during Sunday morning, before tapering to snow showers and flurries Sunday afternoon and evening. However, another period of light snow produced an additional 1 to 2 inches of snowfall across portions of the region Sunday night. The snow ended by Monday morning.

A cold west to northwest flow of air traversing the Great Lakes, combined with the passage of an upper level disturbance, led to numerous snow showers and embedded heavier snow squalls across portions of the southwest Adirondacks, the central and eastern Mohawk Valley, the Schoharie Valley, the Saratoga region and northern portions of the Capital region during Thursday January 8th.||Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with some areas experiencing snowfall rates of between one and two inches per hour. The snow showers and squalls developed during the mid morning hours of Thursday, and tapered off during the early evening hours of Thursday.||Locally heavier snow bands also occurred across portions of southwest Herkimer County, where amounts of up to 7 inches fell.

A low pressure system developed over the lower Mississippi Valley during Tuesday January 27th. The low then strengthened, and tracked northeast from the lower Ohio Valley Wednesday morning on January 28th, into Pennsylvania during Wednesday afternoon, and into northern New England by Thursday morning January 29th.||This storm spread a significant wintry mix of precipitation across eastern New York State, with heavy snow and sleet across much of the southern Adirondacks into the Lake George Saratoga region, with a significant mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain occurring elsewhere.||Snow and sleet amounts ranged from 8 to 12 inches across the southern Adirondacks and the Lake George Saratoga region, with 4 to 8 inches occurring further south across the Mohawk River Valley, the Greater Capital District, the eastern Catskills, the Schoharie Valley, the mid Hudson Valley and into the central and southern Taconics. In addition, ice accretion from freezing rain of between one and three tenths of an inch occurred across areas near and south of the Mohawk River Valley, with locally higher amounts of up to one half of an inch occurring across portions of the mid Hudson Valley.||This wintry mix resulted in the closure of numerous schools and businesses across east central New York for both Wednesday and Thursday mornings, and also created treacherous travel conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold temperatures and wind chills occurred during the morning hours of Friday January 16th. A minimum temperature of -27 F was recorded at Glens Falls Airport.

The combination of gusty winds, and low temperatures during the early morning hours of Thursday January 1st produced wind chills of -15 to |-20 F across the higher elevations of the eastern Catskills and southern Taconics, and -20 to -25 F across the southern Adirondacks, western Mohawk Valley, northern and central Taconics, the Schoharie Valley and Helderbergs, and across the Lake George region.

The combination of gusty winds, and low temperatures during the early morning hours of Thursday January 1st produced wind chills of -15 to |-20 F across the higher elevations of the eastern Catskills and southern Taconics, and -20 to -25 F across the southern Adirondacks, western Mohawk Valley, northern and central Taconics, the Schoharie Valley and Helderbergs, and across the Lake George region.

A low pressure system tracked from the lower Ohio Valley on Tuesday January 6th, into the eastern Great Lakes during Wednesday January 7th, and into northern New England by Thursday morning January 8th. A second low pressure system developed near Long Island during Wednesday, and tracked northeast along the northern New England coast by Thursday morning.||This combination of low pressure systems spread a swath of moderate to heavy precipitation across eastern New York and western New England, beginning late Tuesday evening, and ending early Thursday morning.||The precipitation fell as a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain across the region, before gradually changing to plain rain from south to north during Wednesday as milder air approached from the south. As colder air wrapped back into the region, the precipitation changed to snow before ending later Wednesday night and early Thursday morning.||Snow and sleet amounts ranged from 1 to 4 inches for areas south of the Mohawk River Valley, including within the Capital District, Schoharie Valley, the mid Hudson Valley and eastern Catskills, and the central and southern Taconics, with generally 4 to 8 inches further north, including within the central and eastern Mohawk Valley, and the Lake George and Saratoga region. Locally higher amounts, with 8 to 10 inches, fell across portions of the southern Adirondacks.||In addition, ice accretion from freezing rain generally ranged from one tenth, to three tenths of an inch, with the greatest amounts occurring within the immediate Capital Region north into the eastern Mohawk Valley.||This wintry mix led to widespread hazardous travel conditions during Wednesday morning. In addition, numerous closures, or delayed openings of schools and businesses occurred Wednesday morning.

A low pressure system tracked east from the eastern Great Lakes Sunday morning January 18th, across northern New England Sunday afternoon and evening, to a position off the northern New England coast by Monday morning January 19th.||An area of light to moderate snowfall accompanied the passage of this system across much of east central New York and adjacent western New England from Sunday morning, into early Monday morning.||A narrow swath of heavier snow fell across portions of the Saratoga region, where 8 to 10 of snow accumulated. Elsewhere, snowfall amounts generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with lighter amounts falling within the immediate Capital District.||The steadiest snowfall occurred during Sunday morning, before tapering to snow showers and flurries Sunday afternoon and evening. However, another period of light snow produced an additional 1 to 2 inches of snowfall across portions of the region Sunday night. The snow ended by Monday morning.

An intense area of low pressure over the Eastern Great Lakes on the morning of February 12, tracked northeast and up into the Canadian Maritimes by evening. High winds developed near daybreak, right behind the cold front, and continued through the day. The strongest winds occurred during the morning and appeared to be associated with sharp pressure rises right behind the cold front. ||There were several thousand power outages across southeast New York, a death of a construction worker, and delays of up to 3 hours at the metropolitan area airports. ||The death occurred at approximately 0832EST at a construction site in the Rosebank section of Staten Island. High winds knocked down a cinder block wall on top of a 59-year-old male construction worker.||The following are some peak wind gusts across southeast New York: Bronx County, Bronx at 59 mph; Kings County, Brooklyn at 65 mph, Queens County, 55 mph at JFK airport and 56 mph at LaGuardia airport; Nassau County, 56 mph at Massapequa; Suffolk County, 60 mph at Islip; Westchester County, 55 mph at White Plains; and Orange County, 58 mph at Warwick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long Island Railroad grade crossing gate at the Broadway crossing in Bethpage blown into a passing train, injuring 4 people by shattered glass.

A low pressure system developed over the lower Mississippi Valley during Tuesday January 27th. The low then strengthened, and tracked northeast from the lower Ohio Valley Wednesday morning on January 28th, into Pennsylvania during Wednesday afternoon, and into northern New England by Thursday morning January 29th.||This storm spread a significant wintry mix of precipitation across eastern New York State, with heavy snow and sleet across much of the southern Adirondacks into the Lake George Saratoga region, with a significant mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain occurring elsewhere.||Snow and sleet amounts ranged from 8 to 12 inches across the southern Adirondacks and the Lake George Saratoga region, with 4 to 8 inches occurring further south across the Mohawk River Valley, the Greater Capital District, the eastern Catskills, the Schoharie Valley, the mid Hudson Valley and into the central and southern Taconics. A strengthening low pressure system tracked northeast from the southeast coast on Sunday morning, March 1st, to just southeast of Long Island by Monday morning March 2nd. The low then headed northeast into the Canadian maritimes by Tuesday March 3rd.||This storm system spread a swath of moderate to heavy snow across the mid Hudson Valley, the eastern Catskills, and Taconics, beginning early Monday morning, and ending by late Monday afternoon.||Total snowfall accumulations ranged from 4 to 9 inches across the mid Hudson Valley, southeast Catskills and southern Taconics, with generally 4 inches or less further north and west.||This snowfall led to treacherous travel conditions for the Monday morning commute, with numerous accidents reported. In addition, several closures, or delayed openings of schools and businesses occurred Monday morning due to the snowfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An accident occurred at approximately 1425 LST on Route 155 in New Scotland, resulting in the death of a 69 year old woman. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense area of low pressure over the Eastern Great Lakes on the morning of February 12, tracked northeast and up into the Canadian Maritimes by evening. High winds developed near daybreak, right behind the cold front, and continued through the day. The strongest winds occurred during the morning and appeared to be associated with sharp pressure rises right behind the cold front. ||There were several thousand power outages across southeast New York, a death of a construction worker, and delays of up to 3 hours at the metropolitan area airports. ||The death occurred at approximately 0832EST at a construction site in the Rosebank section of Staten Island. High winds knocked down a cinder block wall on top of a 59-year-old male construction worker.||The following are some peak wind gusts across southeast New York: Bronx County, Bronx at 59 mph; Kings County, Brooklyn at 65 mph, Queens County, 55 mph at JFK airport and 56 mph at LaGuardia airport; Nassau County, 56 mph at Massapequa; Suffolk County, 60 mph at Islip; Westchester County, 55 mph at White Plains; and Orange County, 58 mph at Warwick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds knocked down a cinder block wall, killing a 59-year-old male construction worker at approximately 0832EST in the Rosebank section of Staten Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system tracked northeast from the mid Mississippi Valley Wednesday February 18th, into the lower Saint Lawrence Valley by Thursday morning February 19th. An occluded front associated with this low pressure system swept northeast across eastern New York State early Thursday morning, preceded and accompanied by moderate to locally heavy wet snow.||The heaviest snowfall amounts occurred across the Lake George Saratoga region, where 6 to 10 inches fell. Elsewhere, snowfall amounts generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches across the western and central Mohawk Valley, the greater Capital District, and eastern Catskills, with 4 to 8 inches across portions of the southwest Adirondacks.||The snow began Wednesday afternoon, and tapered off early Thursday morning.

A cold front approached from the west Sunday afternoon and evening on May 24th, preceded and accompanied by scattered thunderstorms, some of which contained damaging wind gusts and large hail. In addition, locally heavy rain produced isolated flash flooding near Wappingers Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported near Clove during a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached from the west Sunday afternoon and evening on May 24th, preceded and accompanied by scattered thunderstorms, some of which contained damaging wind gusts and large hail. In addition, locally heavy rain produced isolated flash flooding near Wappingers Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported in Knapps Corner, located approximately 1 mile west of Dutchess County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavy rainfall, snowmelt and ice jams led to flooding across portions of the western Mohawk Valley, from Saturday evening on March 7th, to Sunday morning on March 8th. The heavy rainfall and milder temperatures were the result of the passage of a low pressure system, which tracked from the upper Ohio Valley into northern New England from late Saturday into Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed due to flooding in the vicinity of Saint Johnsville. In addition, the Canajoharie Creek reached moderate flood stage at 1330 LST on March 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal low pressure rapidly intensified Saturday night as it tracked northeast from off the Delmarva Coast...just southeast of the 40N/70W benchmark���and to southeast of Cape Cod by Sunday morning. This was in response to a powerful upper low approaching and then crossing south of the area. The track of low pressure to the southeast of the region and stubborn placement of arctic high pressure over the Northern US Plains and Southern Canadian Plains...ridging eastward...made for the ideal setup for heavy snow across most of the region. The rapid intensification of low pressure created blizzard or near blizzard conditions over Long Island for several hours with northeast winds of 15 to 25 mph and gusts 35 to 45 mph���and visibilities below �� mile. Near whiteout conds were experienced across Suffolk County during the height of the storm. Snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches per hour occurred with an intense mesoscale band that developed S of the region���pivoted northwest into West/Central Long Island���and then slowly pulled east through the overnight. Snow totals increased rapidly from northwest to southeast across the region...with 3 to 6 inches across Orange County���around 1 ft across the NYC metro���and around 2 ft across Suffolk County.

Deep low pressure moved across southern Ontario and Lake Ontario to the New England coast. The track and intensity of the low resulted in high winds for much of western New York. Wind gusts were measured to 69 mph with widespread estimated gusts between 55 and 60 mph. The strong winds downed trees and power lines. Electric utilities reported nearly 100,000 customers without power at the peak of the storm. Specific measured wind gusts included: 69 mph in Dunkirk; 62 mph in Silver Creek; 62 mph in Angola, Yorkshire and Sherman; 60 mph in Warsaw and Barker; 59 mph in Hamburg; and 58 mph in Westfield and LeRoy.
As a low pressure system strengthened off the East Coast, a cold northwesterly flow of air brought lake effect snow southeast of Lake Ontario. Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 9 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow bands brought around 8 inches of snow to northern parts of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow bands brought around 4 to 9 inches of snow to northern parts of the county. Slaterville Springs measured 8.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving from the Midwestern states across Lake Ontario brought strong winds to the lake plain counties in west-central New York. Sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph developed, with gusts in excess of 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 65 mph was measured at the Penn Yan Airport by ASOS equipment. Several limbs and wires were blown down throughout the county.

An intensifying area of low pressure in the Central Plains on March 10th, tracked across the Northern Great Lakes and into Hudson Bay on March 11th.  Meanwhile, an area of surface high pressure was located  across Eastern Canada and this allowed for a strong pressure gradient across New York.||A strong low-level jet aloft preceded an approaching cold front on  March 11th and eventually lowered to the surface during the afternoon. Surface winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts in excess of 50 mph were observed with scattered tree damage and very isolated property damage.

An unseasonably strong spring storm moved across the northern Plains into central Canada on May 13th and 14th. At the same time, a strong surface high was located across New England on May 13th and strengthened while it slowly exited south of Nova Scotia on May 14th. This created a strong pressure gradient across northern New York. In addition, a strong mid-level disturbance moved across the Great Lakes and through northern New York and Vermont on the 14th.||Strong south to southwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with frequent gusts in excess of 50 mph was witnessed across much of northern New York during the morning and early afternoon hours of May 14th. These winds resulted in numerous reports of scattered downed trees, as the spring season accounted for foliage on the trees. There were some localized higher gusts that approached 60 mph that caused some minor structural damage (roof shingles and poorly structured porches), as well as more numerous tree damage in portions of Franklin and western Clinton counties.

Deep low pressure moved across southern Ontario and Lake Ontario to the New England coast. The track and intensity of the low resulted in high winds for much of western New York. Wind gusts were measured to 69 mph with widespread estimated gusts between 55 and 60 mph. The strong winds downed trees and power lines. Electric utilities reported nearly 100,000 customers without power at the peak of the storm. Specific measured wind gusts included: 69 mph in Dunkirk; 62 mph in Silver Creek; 62 mph in Angola, Yorkshire and Sherman; 60 mph in Warsaw and Barker; 59 mph in Hamburg; and 58 mph in Westfield and LeRoy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system tracked northeast from the southeast coast on Sunday morning, March 1st, to just southeast of Long Island by Monday morning March 2nd. The low then headed northeast into the Canadian maritimes by Tuesday March 3rd.||This storm system spread a swath of moderate to heavy snow across the mid Hudson Valley, the eastern Catskills, and Taconics, beginning early Monday morning, and ending by late Monday afternoon.||Total snowfall accumulations ranged from 4 to 9 inches across the mid Hudson Valley, southeast Catskills and southern Taconics, with generally 4 inches or less further north and west.||This snowfall led to treacherous travel conditions for the Monday morning commute, with numerous accidents reported. In addition, several closures, or delayed openings of schools and businesses occurred Monday morning due to the snowfall.

An intensifying area of low pressure in the Central Plains on March 10th, tracked across the Northern Great Lakes and into Hudson Bay on March 11th.  Meanwhile, an area of surface high pressure was located  across Eastern Canada and this allowed for a strong pressure gradient across New York.||A strong low-level jet aloft preceded an approaching cold front on  March 11th and eventually lowered to the surface during the afternoon. Surface winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts in excess of 50 mph were observed with scattered tree damage and very isolated property damage.

The combination of heavy rainfall, snowmelt and ice jams led to flooding across portions of the western Mohawk Valley, from Saturday evening on March 7th, to Sunday morning on March 8th. The heavy rainfall and milder temperatures were the result of the passage of a low pressure system, which tracked from the upper Ohio Valley into northern New England from late Saturday into Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along Route 5S in the village of German Flatts, resulting in a road closure. Emergency officials reported chunks of ice and water up to 2 feet deep on the road, over a distance of 100 feet. Additional flooding occurred from runoff and snowmelt from the Nowadaga Creek in the vicinity of Route 5S and Creek Road in the town of Danube, resulting in another road closure.||Several other roads were closed due to flooding, including Partridge Hill Road in Russia, and Broad Street in the town of Frankfort. In addition, the Barge Canal overflowed onto Southern Avenue in the vicinity of State Route 167 in Little Falls.||More roads were closed in the vicinity of Dolgeville due to an ice jam on the East Canada Creek. The ice jam caused water to back up onto Dolge Avenue, and also resulted in the flooding of several homes.

The ice jam stretched from the Freemans Bridge, west to Lock 8 in Rotterdam.  The ice jam was freed by Monday afternoon.

On the afternoon of June 26th, an unseasonably cold and strong upper level low pressure system was located across eastern Canada. Mid-level shortwave energy rotated around this upper low into a moderately unstable air mass across northern New York and Vermont. This resulted in widespread thunderstorm activity, some of which produced large hail and brief strong winds across portions of northern New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported at North County Golf Club.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 9th, a surface low and cold front moved from the Great Lakes across northern New York and Vermont, along a stationary boundary draped across the Adirondacks and central Vermont. This stationary boundary separated the cool, moist and stable air to the north, from the warm, moist and unstable air south. In addition a long-lived, strong mid-level shortwave and associated thunderstorm complex traveled across the Great Lakes and along this boundary across New York during the afternoon hours. ||Severe thunderstorms and a developing squall line produced large hail up to an inch in diameter as well as damaging winds that knocked down trees and power lines to portions of southern St. Lawrence and Essex counties. Aerial photographs of tree damage in far northwest Essex county (9 NW Newcomb) showed evidence of possible micro burst activity as a hundred or so trees were flattened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low pressure system along the Outer Banks of North Carolina Sunday evening, March 1, tracked northeast, to east of Long Island by Monday afternoon, March 2.  At the same time, arctic high pressure, from the upper midwest to James Bay, was gradually building east.  The two combined to produce a heavy snowfall event across the tri-state area with snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches an hour during the pre-dawn hours of March 2. This was the biggest snowfall event of the winter season.||Snowfall amounts across southeast New York generally ranged from 7 to 12 inches, but as high as 16 inches across Suffolk county on eastern Long Island.  The following are selected snowfall amounts across the area:  11.2 inches at Tuxedo in the Lower Hudson Valley, 13.0 inches at Great Kills on Staten Island, 8.0 incheas at the Central Park Zoo in Manhattan, and 15.7 inches at Mastic on eastern Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 57-year-old male was killed when he lost control of his vehicle on an icy road and was struck broadside by a van at approximately 2109 EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of June 25th, a weak mid-level disturbance moved across a marginally unstable air mass, with temperatures in the 80s and dew points in the 60s, across northern New York. This resulted in scattered thunderstorms, including an isolated and brief severe thunderstorm that produced up to quarter size hail and estimated wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph that toppled a few wooden benches at Schroon Lake golf course. Also, nickel size hail was reported in downtown Schroon Lake at the Post Office.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail covered the ground with some hail stones as large as a nickel.

A tight pressure gradient between high pressure off the New England coast, and a low pressure system located north of the Great Lakes, produced strong south to southeast winds across portions of eastern New York from late Thursday morning on May 14th, until the mid afternoon hours. A cold front associated with the low then crossed the region during the late afternoon hours, ending the strong winds.||Measured wind gusts were generally between 35 and 50 mph across the immediate Hudson River Valley from Albany northward, as well as across the Mohawk Valley and southwest Adirondacks. However, locally stronger gusts were estimated in portions of southern Herkimer County, where numerous trees and wires were downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several dozen trees, in addition to wires, were reported down across southern Herkimer County as a result of strong winds. The most numerous downed trees and wires were reported in the vicinity of Columbia, Warren, Orendorf Corners, Frankfort, German Flatts, Schuyler Corners, and Cedarville. ||One tree fell onto a car in the town of Frankfort, while another tree fell onto a house in Schuyler Corners.||In addition, a 200 pound grill reportedly rolled over from the strong winds in Frankfort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold high pressure remained over the region. For two consecutive nights, clear skies and light winds resulted in temperatures dropping well into the 20's in some spots. The cold temperatures damaged early season vine crops including berries and grapes. Agricultural damages from the two-night freezes were estimated near $5 million. A strong, cool mid-atmospheric shortwave delivered a cold front across northern New York during the evening of July 11th. A line of thunderstorms developed along this cold front in southeast Ontario province during the evening and proceeded across northern New York. There was numerous lightning, heavy rain and gusty winds that accompanied these storms, but only an isolated report of localized wind damage in the Winthrop vicinity in St. Lawrence county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large trees and power lines downed by thunderstorm winds along Brookdale Road and neighboring streets in Winthrop. Trees damaged a few vehicles and small structures.

The combination of a passing upper level trough, and unusually cold air in the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere, led to the development of numerous thunderstorms across eastern New York during the late morning into the early evening hours on Monday June 15th, many of which contained large quantities of hail. The thunderstorms developed during the late morning hours, and continued through early evening.||In addition, some thunderstorms were slow moving, and produced locally very intense rainfall rates. This led to flash flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported in Kinderhook during a thunderstorm. In addition, a peak wind gust of 41 mph was measured.

A cold front moved east across New York state during the evening. Ahead of the front, showers and thunderstorms developed. Two of the thunderstorms produced tornadoes, with other scattered severe thunderstorms producing reports of wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team determined that a severe thunderstorm produced straight-line wind gusts to 80 mph across portions of southeast Chemung county. Some of the damage that was observed included damage to the roof of an apartment building on Wynkoop Creek Road, and extensive tree damage along a portion of West Drybrook Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved east across New York state during the evening. Ahead of the front, showers and thunderstorms developed. Two of the thunderstorms produced tornadoes, with other scattered severe thunderstorms producing reports of wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm team surveyed damage produced by a severe thunderstorm that occurred around 5 pm. It was determined that a tornado touched down near Route 34, about 2 miles north of Waverly in southwest Tioga county. The determination was based on eye witness reports, and a small area of convergent wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved east across New York state during the evening. Ahead of the front, showers and thunderstorms developed. Two of the thunderstorms produced tornadoes, with other scattered severe thunderstorms producing reports of wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team determined that a weak tornado touched down briefly near Cuyler. Some trees were uprooted and two small sheds were destroyed. Greater damage occurred to the west at a couple of farms and several residences between Truxton and Cuyler, but this damage was determined to be caused by straight line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved east across New York state during the evening. Ahead of the front, showers and thunderstorms developed. Two of the thunderstorms produced tornadoes, with other scattered severe thunderstorms producing reports of wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team determined that damage in the county was caused by a severe thunderstorm that produced a tornado. Most of the damage along the path was to trees. Significant damage occurred approximately 1 mile south of the Village of Georgetown. A large barn was destroyed, a roof was torn off a house, several outbuildings and sheds were destroyed, and power poles were snapped. There was one injury at this location. A second farm on Upham Road was hit, destroying a large barn, a silo and a couple of sheds. Some livestock were lost at this location. A farmstead near Niles Road also received damage to a silo, and damage to the home's roof and siding.

An unusually strong low pressure system crossed southern Ontario and brought strong winds to western New York. Winds gusts were measured between 50 and 55 mph at Buffalo and Niagara Falls. At Rochester airport a gust of 56 mph was measured. The windy conditions in Rochester interrupted the annual Lilac festival. A large tent erected for the graduation ceremonies at Nazareth College was blown down by the wind. Four people (three maintenance workers and one guest) suffered minor injuries in the collapse. The funneling effects of Lake Erie also resulted in downed trees and power lines across parts of Chautauqua county. In Cherry Creek, a large tree fell onto a house and two cars. In Falconer, a trailer was damaged by a downed tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low pressure system along the Outer Banks of North Carolina Sunday evening, March 1, tracked northeast, to east of Long Island by Monday afternoon, March 2.  At the same time, arctic high pressure, from the upper midwest to James Bay, was gradually building east.  The two combined to produce a heavy snowfall event across the tri-state area with snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches an hour during the pre-dawn hours of March 2. This was the biggest snowfall event of the winter season.||Snowfall amounts across southeast New York generally ranged from 7 to 12 inches, but as high as 16 inches across Suffolk county on eastern Long Island.  The following are selected snowfall amounts across the area:  11.2 inches at Tuxedo in the Lower Hudson Valley, 13.0 inches at Great Kills on Staten Island, 8.0 incheas at the Central Park Zoo in Manhattan, and 15.7 inches at Mastic on eastern Long Island.

A cold front moved east across New York state during the evening. Ahead of the front, showers and thunderstorms developed. Two of the thunderstorms produced tornadoes, with other scattered severe thunderstorms producing reports of wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down.

A vigorous and unseasonably cold storm system traversed across Ontario and Quebec provinces on May 31st. This translated into a strong mid-level disturbance and low-level jet stream that supported a sharp, strong cold front. ||This strong cold front moved across northern New York during the afternoon and resulted in a brief period of strong and gusty winds of 40 to 50 mph along with a 20 degree temperature plunge. These strong winds accounted for numerous reports of fallen trees and scattered power outages. There were numerous reports of pea-size hail across northern New York and several reports of light snow in the Adirondacks.

The two combined to produce a heavy snowfall event across the tri-state area with snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches an hour during the pre-dawn hours of March 2. This was the biggest snowfall event of the winter season.||Snowfall amounts across southeast New York generally ranged from 7 to 12 inches, but as high as 16 inches across Suffolk county on eastern Long Island.  The following are selected snowfall amounts across the area:  11.2 inches at Tuxedo in the Lower Hudson Valley, 13.0 inches at Great Kills on Staten Island, 8.0 incheas at the Central Park Zoo in Manhattan, and 15.7 inches at Mastic on eastern Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The snowstorm grounded 115 flights at Kennedy Airport and 450 at LaGuardia Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous and unseasonably cold storm system traversed across Ontario and Quebec provinces on May 31st. This translated into a strong mid-level disturbance and low-level jet stream that supported a sharp, strong cold front. ||This strong cold front moved across northern New York during the afternoon and resulted in a brief period of strong and gusty winds of 40 to 50 mph along with a 20 degree temperature plunge. These strong winds accounted for numerous reports of fallen trees and scattered power outages. There were numerous reports of pea-size hail across northern New York and several reports of light snow in the Adirondacks.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches were broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave rotating around a deep upper trough over New England acted on warm humid air in place at the surface to allow for supercell thunderstorms to form and track across the Lower Hudson Valley and Long Island.  These storms were prolific hail producers and also caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter Size hail was reported in Newburgh.

An upper level trough and associated surface low pressure system slowly advanced towards the local area from the Ohio Valley triggering scattered thunderstorms with heavy rain which caused flash flooding on several main-stem highways from Pleasantville to Pelham Manor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both directions of the Bronx River Pkwy. from the Sprain Brook Pkwy. in Yonkers to Route 119/Main St. in White Plains were closed due to flooding.  One car was submerged in high water (approximately 3 to 4 ft high) on the parkway in Bronxville. Both directions of the Hutchinson River Pkwy. between Boston Post Rd. and the Cross County Pkwy. and the Saw Mill River Pkwy from Exit 27, Marble Ave. to Exit 30, Grand Street were also closed due to flooding.  Flooding also closed the Cross County Pkwy. at the Exit 9 ramp to the Hutchinson River Pkwy.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 35-year old man drowned in East Rockaway Inlet, about 300 yards offshore from Far Rockway beaches, after being impacted by a rip-current.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance brought a round of thunderstorms to the Finger Lakes region during the early afternoon hours. Hail up to an inch and a half was reported and the thunderstorms winds downed trees and power lines. Several thousand customers lost power because of the storm. Poor drainage flooding was reported. The LPGA tournament at Locust Hill Country Club was temporarily suspended by the storms.

A warm front brought heavy rains to the western southern tier of New York. Portions of Chautauqua County received upwards of two inches of rain in about two hours. Several local roads were damaged by the flash flooding that resulted. Specific roads included: Frew Run Road and Old Warren Road in the town of Carroll; Hunt Road and Fairmount Avenue in Lakewood; and West Perimeter Road in Onoville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of an upper level low pressure system, extreme instability in place, along with a cold front approaching from the west, triggered numerous thunderstorms across eastern New York during Tuesday afternoon and evening on June 30th, some of which became severe.||In addition, some thunderstorms redeveloped over the same areas, producing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel size hail was reported near Lake Katrine during a thunderstorm. Initially, penny size hail was reported, then nickel size hail was reported at 1431 LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of an upper level low pressure system, extreme instability in place, along with a cold front approaching from the west, triggered numerous thunderstorms across eastern New York during Tuesday afternoon and evening on June 30th, some of which became severe.||In addition, some thunderstorms redeveloped over the same areas, producing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reported down in Queensbury due to strong thunderstorm winds. Trees were also reported down on Sunnyside Road East near Queensbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of an upper level low pressure system, extreme instability in place, along with a cold front approaching from the west, triggered numerous thunderstorms across eastern New York during Tuesday afternoon and evening on June 30th, some of which became severe.||In addition, some thunderstorms redeveloped over the same areas, producing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route 10 near Palatine Bridge was closed due to flash flooding. In addition, rushing floodwaters blasted out the basement windows of the United Methodist Church on Main Street in Canajoharie, inundating the basement with at least 8 inches of water.||Flood water also filled sewers that carry a steam line between the Beech Nut plant on Church Street and the Richardson Brands buildings on both sides of Erie Boulevard, which resulted in a minor rupture of a pipe at the Richardson Brands plant.||Furthermore, a small creek overflowed its banks along Happy Hollow Road near Fort Plain, resulting in the erosion of nearly 4 feet of earth from beneath the foundation of a garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 16th, a strong upper atmospheric area of low pressure was located north of the Great Lakes in south central Canada. A significant mid-level shortwave and associated surface low moved across the eastern Great Lakes, while a warm front and subsequent cold front swept across northern New York during the afternoon.||Scattered thunderstorms developed across northern New York, in a moderately unstable atmosphere during the afternoon, with a few storms that produced large hail or strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A female standing by a third floor window at 1 Cedar Lane was struck and injured by lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough caused scattered severe thunderstorms to develop across Southeast New York, including a tornado in Orange County. Some of the storms produced very heavy rain which led to flash flooding in New York City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on a damage survey conducted by the NWS Mount Holly New Jersey Office in conjunction with the Orange County Office of Emergency Management, along with evidence from Doppler Weather Radar and photographs, it was confirmed that an EF0 tornado struck Unionville. Dozens of trees and a multitude of large tree branches fell across streets, and upon several houses and cars.|  |This tornado was produced by the same severe thunderstorm that produced an EF2 tornado in Wantage New Jersey just minutes earlier during the afternoon on July 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front transiting the Tri-State triggered multiple severe thunderstorms across Southeastern New York, especially across northern portions of the Lower Hudson Valley and Eastern Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn roof was damaged on the Karsten Family Farm along South Kaisertown Road.  It also knocked crates of cabbage off a truck onto nearby fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado crossed the town of Darien and village of Corfu in Genesee county. The initial touchdown occurred at 453 PM EDT on Reynolds Road in Darien. The tornado moved northeast across Routes 77 and 33 before lifting near Boyce Road at 505 PM EDT (a path length of about four miles). Widespread damage occurred to the small village. Over 30 homes were damaged and three buildings were demolished. Two greenhouses were also destroyed. Twenty power poles were snapped, countless trees were uprooted and a number of vehicles sustained damage. The tornado was rated an EF1 with wind speeds estimated close to 100 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado crossed the town of Darien and village of Corfu in Genesee county. The initial touchdown occurred at 453 PM EDT on Reynolds Road in Darien. The tornado moved northeast across Routes 77 and 33 before lifting near Boyce Road at 505 PM EDT (a path length of about four miles). Widespread damage occurred to the small village. Over 30 homes were damaged and three buildings were demolished. Two greenhouses were also destroyed. Twenty power poles were snapped, countless trees were uprooted and a number of vehicles sustained damage. The tornado was rated an EF1 with wind speeds estimated close to 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak mid-atmospheric disturbance moved along a stationary front across the eastern Great Lakes into western New York during the afternoon of August 11th. The air mass this traveled across was marginally unstable and thus produced isolated thunderstorms. One thunderstorm briefly pulsed upwards in intensity and produced an isolated wind gust that knocked down several branches and a tree in the town of Gouverneur, which resulted in approximately 250 residents without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines and one tree downed by thunderstorm winds on Grove street...approximately 250 customers without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in a southerly flow of warm, moist, unstable air. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail. Trees and wires were downed. In Webster, a tree fell on a house. Hail, up to 1 inch in diameter, was reported at the Buffalo Airport and in Harris Hill and Clarence Center.

Showers and thunderstorms developed during the mid afternoon to evening hours ahead of an approaching cold front. Several of the thunderstorms were severe, producing reports of both large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding from heavy rain caused one structure to collapse and one home was flooded. Streets were flooded in Otego. Interstate 88 was closed temporarily due to mud flow. Radar indicated an area of 2.50 to 4.00 inches of rain fell as far as 4 miles north and east of Otego.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms brought locally heavy rainfall to parts of the western Catskills. With the ground already saturated from rain that fell over the preceding days, additional flooding problems occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A county official  reported that several homes were evacuated as route 164 was flooded. A National Weather Service storm survey team confirmed that state route 164 was covered with water due to a brook that over-topped the road. This brook is a tributary to the east branch of Callicoon Creek. Houses were also flooded near Kohlertown due to a combination of flooding from the brook mentioned above along with the east branch of the Callicoon creek running over its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of high pressure to the northeast and a developing coastal low to the south produced  strong northeast winds over the region, piling water up in the Back Bays of Long Island.  Coastal flooding was measured at Freeport and observed at Lindenhurst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS tidal gauge at Freeport measured a water level at or above 5 ft. NGVD (the threshold for moderate flooding) from 9:33-10:57 pm local time.  This is an observed surge of 1.9 ft. at Freeport.

A very powerful low pressure system (980mb) moved across the Great Lakes into Ontario province Canada on December 9th. Despite the strength of this storm, impacts were marginal across northern New York with snow, blowing snow and mixed wintry precipitation. Although, it did provide the first widespread snowfall for the 2009-10 winter season.||Snowfall amounts ranged from several inches in the St. Lawrence River Valley and northern Champlain Valley to more than 6 inches across portions of the Adirondacks and central Champlain Valley. Some specific totals included 9 inches in Newcomb (Essex county) and 6 inches in Peru (Clinton county).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably cold Canadian high pressure built across the lower Great Lakes region and settled over New York State. Mainly clear skies, light winds and plenty of cold, dry air associated with this system provided ideal conditions for a hard freeze to occur. Overnight lows ranged from the mid 20s to near the freezing mark closer to the lakeshores.  A sampling of minimum temperatures included:  32 at Fulton; 31 at Port Byron; 29 at Orchard Park, Lyndonville, Rochester, and Alden; 28 at Yorkshire, Sherman and Rose; 27 at Attica, Bristol; 26 at Turin; 25 at Ellicottville, Watertown and 24 at Andover and Pendleton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm that produced storm damage in Bergen county in|northeastern New Jersey moved into Yonkers around 11:25 pm as the|storm intensified.  Doppler weather radar analysis would classify|the storm as a supercell with a strong mesocyclone at this time. A|large area of very strong downburst winds downed numerous large|trees that fell on structures, power lines and cars. The worst damage|observed in the area was in Trevor Park around the Hudson River|Museum where winds were estimated to have gusted up to 100 mph. Some minor damage appeared to occur to the roof of the Museum with a few shingles blown off. Nearly all trees appeared to have fallen to the east or southeast. The damage area extended east to North Broadway and into Grant Park on Park Ave where numerous large trees were also knocked over falling to the east or southeast. Downed large trees were also observed in Oakland Cemetery.||Downed trees were also observed in Mount Vernon along West Street|and Macquesten Parkway North where winds of 70 mph were estimated to|have occurred. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive convective complex moved from lower Michigan across southern Ontario near Toronto then dove southeast across the Niagara Frontier and Western Southern Tier. This followed an earlier round of strong thunderstorms and heavy rains earlier in the day. Damage from the thunderstorm winds was widespread across the Niagara Frontier and Western Southern Tier.  Trees as large as two to three feet in diameter were downed. Power outages were scattered throughout the region as the winds downed power lines.|The unprecedented heavy rains that fell���four to six inches in less than two hours���resulted in some of the worst flash flooding the area has seen. Parts of Erie, Chautauqua, Cattaraugus and Allegany counties received State and Federal Disaster Declarations. In the Village of Silver Creek an entire mobile home park along Silver Creek was washed away. The flood waters continued through the heart of the village through the fire station, police headquarters and highway department. Several police cars, an ambulance and much of the town���s highway equipment were lost. In Irving, the flood waters flowed across parts of the New York State Thruway and Routes 5 and 20. Water flooded portions of Lakeshore Hospital there. Also in Irving, the Seneca Nation sustained almost two million dollars in damage to roads and bridges. In total, in Chautauqua county hundreds of homes were damaged and 45 homes were condemned, including the 35 mobile homes of the aforementioned park. In the Village of Gowanda (part of which is in Erie County and part in Cattaraugus county), extensive flash flooding occurred. Tri-County hospital in Gowanda was inundated by flood waters. Patients were transferred to Lakeshore Hospital in Irving. Several hundred homes sustained damage and 40 homes were condemned there. One fatality occurred in Gowanda as an 80-year-old man ventured out to check a bridge on his property and was swept away by the flood waters. The gage on Cattaraugus Creek at Gowanda showed a rise from six feet to near 13 feet in less than an hour (flood stage is 10 feet). Throughout Cattaraugus county over one-quarter of the roads were damaged or lost.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably cold Canadian high pressure built across the lower Great Lakes region and settled over New York State. Mainly clear skies, light winds and plenty of cold, dry air associated with this system provided ideal conditions for a hard freeze to occur. Overnight lows ranged from the mid 20s to near the freezing mark closer to the lakeshores.  A sampling of minimum temperatures included:  32 at Fulton; 31 at Port Byron; 29 at Orchard Park, Lyndonville, Rochester, and Alden; 28 at Yorkshire, Sherman and Rose; 27 at Attica, Bristol; 26 at Turin; 25 at Ellicottville, Watertown and 24 at Andover and Pendleton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably cold Canadian high pressure built across the lower Great Lakes region and settled over New York State. Mainly clear skies, light winds and plenty of cold, dry air associated with this system provided ideal conditions for a hard freeze to occur. Overnight lows ranged from the mid 20s to near the freezing mark closer to the lakeshores.  A sampling of minimum temperatures included:  32 at Fulton; 31 at Port Byron; 29 at Orchard Park, Lyndonville, Rochester, and Alden; 28 at Yorkshire, Sherman and Rose; 27 at Attica, Bristol; 26 at Turin; 25 at Ellicottville, Watertown and 24 at Andover and Pendleton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching frontal boundary brought scattered showers and thunderstorms to central New York. A few of the storms became severe across the Mohawk Valley and Catskills, and produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down, blocking State Route 8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monthly rainfall well above average continued during the month of July causing significant crop damage on the farms of the north and south forks of Suffolk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of wet weather continued during the month of July causing significant crop damage, which lead to lost sales in the hundreds of thousands of dollars. The unofficial monthly rainfall total at the historical climate site in Bridgehampton, NY was 6.16 inches which is 3.07 inches above normal for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in a southerly flow of warm, moist, unstable air. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail. Trees and wires were downed. In Webster, a tree fell on a house. Hail, up to 1 inch in diameter, was reported at the Buffalo Airport and in Harris Hill and Clarence Center.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree which fell on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive convective complex moved from lower Michigan across southern Ontario near Toronto then dove southeast across the Niagara Frontier and Western Southern Tier. Damage reports were fairly widespread across the area with downed power lines, limbs and trees. Power outages resulted from the downed power lines. Several homes and automobiles were damaged by felled trees. The heavy rains that accompanied the thunderstorms produced some flash flooding in Chautauqua County. Small creeks overflowed and parking lots were flooded in Jamestown. In Lakewood, numerous roads and viaducts were flooded and closed.

An unseasonably deep area of low pressure over the upper Great Lakes swept a powerful cold front across the Niagara Frontier and western Finger Lakes Region. The thunderstorms which accompanied the cold front produced strong, damaging winds. Gusts were measured to 61 mph. The winds downed trees and power lines throughout the region with utilities reporting tens of thousands of customers without service.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforecment reported trees down near Transit Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A massive convective complex moved from lower Michigan across southern Ontario near Toronto then dove southeast across the Niagara Frontier and Western Southern Tier. This followed an earlier round of strong thunderstorms and heavy rains earlier in the day. Damage from the thunderstorm winds was widespread across the Niagara Frontier and Western Southern Tier.  Trees as large as two to three feet in diameter were downed. Power outages were scattered throughout the region as the winds downed power lines.|The unprecedented heavy rains that fell���four to six inches in less than two hours���resulted in some of the worst flash flooding the area has seen. Parts of Erie, Chautauqua, Cattaraugus and Allegany counties received State and Federal Disaster Declarations. In the Village of Silver Creek an entire mobile home park along Silver Creek was washed away. The flood waters continued through the heart of the village through the fire station, police headquarters and highway department. Several police cars, an ambulance and much of the town���s highway equipment were lost. In Irving, the flood waters flowed across parts of the New York State Thruway and Routes 5 and 20. Water flooded portions of Lakeshore Hospital there. Also in Irving, the Seneca Nation sustained almost two million dollars in damage to roads and bridges. In total, in Chautauqua county hundreds of homes were damaged and 45 homes were condemned, including the 35 mobile homes of the aforementioned park. In the Village of Gowanda (part of which is in Erie County and part in Cattaraugus county), extensive flash flooding occurred. Tri-County hospital in Gowanda was inundated by flood waters. Patients were transferred to Lakeshore Hospital in Irving. Several hundred homes sustained damage and 40 homes were condemned there. One fatality occurred in Gowanda as an 80-year-old man ventured out to check a bridge on his property and was swept away by the flood waters. The gage on Cattaraugus Creek at Gowanda showed a rise from six feet to near 13 feet in less than an hour (flood stage is 10 feet). Throughout Cattaraugus county over one-quarter of the roads were damaged or lost.

Strong thunderstorms accompanied the passage of a cold front during the afternoon hours. The thunderstorms produced hail up to one inch in diameter in Wayne county and strong winds that downed trees and power lines in Jefferson and Lewis counties. The annual Military Appreciation Day at Fort Drum had to be cut short for safety reasons. The winds there blew away a large inflatable slide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave rotating around a deep upper trough over New England acted on warm humid air in place at the surface to allow for supercell thunderstorms to form and track across the Lower Hudson Valley and Long Island.  These storms were prolific hail producers and also caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees down on wires were reported in Otisville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms preceded and accompanied a cold front moving across the region during the late morning and afternoon hours. The thunderstorm winds downed power lines in the town of Caledonia in Livingston county.

The combination of a slow moving cold front approaching from the west, and an abundantly moist and warm air mass in place, led to the development of numerous showers and thunderstorms across eastern New York and western New England during Wednesday afternoon and evening on July 29th.||Some of the thunderstorms were severe, producing isolated damaging winds.  In addition, several clusters of thunderstorms moved slowly, and trained across the same areas for several hours, producing torrential rainfall and resulting in significant flash flooding.||Trained spotters measured rainfall amounts of 4 to 7 inches within the most persistent thunderstorms over a 3 to 6 hour period, particularly near Kinderhook, and adjacent areas of northern Columbia and southern Rensselaer Counties. In addition to widespread flash flooding, the Kinderhook Creek also overflowed its banks, resulting in additional flash flooding.||As a result of the flooding, Amtrak train service was suspended early Thursday morning on July 30th between the Rensselaer Rail Station and Penn Station, in order for track and signal systems to be examined for flood damage in Hudson. The rail service resumed by early Thursday afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall across eastern portions of Greene County Wednesday afternoon and evening, resulting in flash flooding. State Route 385, or Mansion Street, in Coxsackie was closed due to flash flooding from this heavy rainfall. Several other streets within the village of Coxsackie were also impassable Wednesday evening due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of an upper level low pressure system, extreme instability in place, along with a cold front approaching from the west, triggered numerous thunderstorms across eastern New York during Tuesday afternoon and evening on June 30th, some of which became severe.||In addition, some thunderstorms redeveloped over the same areas, producing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported on St. Johnsville during a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving frontal system brought scattered showers and thunderstorms to the region. Isolated storms produced strong winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed about 1/2 mile from the location of damage in Bethel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a passing upper level trough, and unusually cold air in the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere, led to the development of numerous thunderstorms across eastern New York during the late morning into the early evening hours on Monday June 15th, many of which contained large quantities of hail. The thunderstorms developed during the late morning hours, and continued through early evening.||In addition, some thunderstorms were slow moving, and produced locally very intense rainfall rates. This led to flash flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported in Ames during a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Soaking rains over a four-day period beginning on the 7th provided the region with nearly all of the month���s precipitation���between 2 and 3 inches. The rain combined with the melting winter snowpack and frozen ground to result in widespread flooding on area rivers and creeks. There were numerous reports of road closures along those rivers and creeks. This was the third winter flood event this season���a rare occurrence in western New York. |Specific river/creek crest information follows:  Cazenovia Creek at Ebenezer (flood stage 10 feet) crested at 11.51 feet at 3AM on the 8th. Cayuga Creek at Lancaster (flood stage 8 feet) crested at 9.31 feet at 5AM on the 8th. Buffalo Creek at Gardenville (flood stage 7 feet) crested at 7.71 feet at 4AM on the 8th. Ellicott Creek at Williamsville (flood stage 8 feet) crested at 8.04 feet at 2AM on the 9th. Black Creek at Churchville (flood stage 6 feet) crested at 6.48 feet at 5PM on the 9th. Oatka Creek at Garbutt (flood stage 6 feet) crested at 6.70 feet at 2PM on the 9th. Allegheny River at Olean (flood stage 10 feet) crested at 10.34 feet at 11AM on the 11th. Tonawanda Creek at Batavia (flood stage 9 feet) crested at 10.57 feet at 11PM on the 8th; and at Rapids (flood stage 12 feet) crested at 13.68 feet at 6AM on the 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system approached the region Thursday, July 16th. A warm front associated with the low moved northeast of the region by early afternoon, placing the local area in a very unstable airmass. A cold front then moved across the area Thursday night. In addition, an upper level low was approaching from the Great Lakes Region. Severe thunderstorms developed across Herkimer County by mid afternoon and moved southeastward across east central New York through the rest of the afternoon into the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported approximately 2 miles northwest of Albany during a thunderstorm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported approximately 5 miles west of Cohoes during a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rather complex weather pattern was in place across the region Friday, July 17th, along with an unstable airmass. An upper level low was over the Great Lakes Region and a disturbance rotating about it triggered thunderstorms across the mid Hudson Valley during the afternoon hours, some of which became severe.||In addition, the cold front which triggered widespread severe weather across eastern New York Thursday the 16th stalled along the eastern seaboard from New England southward to the mid Atlantic Region on Friday, July 17th. A wave of low pressure developed along the boundary and headed northward Friday night triggering a line of thunderstorms which moved northward across Dutchess and Litchfield counties late in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree were reported down on wires in South Dover due to strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper level low and pool of unusually cold air in the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere moved over the region Tuesday, July 7th, leading to the development of numerous thunderstorms across eastern New York, many of which contained large quantities of hail. The severe thunderstorms developed during the late morning hours, and continued through early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed onto wires in Mohawk due to strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a slow moving cold front approaching from the west, and an abundantly moist and warm air mass in place, led to the development of numerous showers and thunderstorms across eastern New York and western New England during Wednesday afternoon and evening on July 29th.||Some of the thunderstorms were severe, producing isolated damaging winds.  In addition, several clusters of thunderstorms moved slowly, and trained across the same areas for several hours, producing torrential rainfall and resulting in significant flash flooding.||Trained spotters measured rainfall amounts of 4 to 7 inches within the most persistent thunderstorms over a 3 to 6 hour period, particularly near Kinderhook, and adjacent areas of northern Columbia and southern Rensselaer Counties. In addition to widespread flash flooding, the Kinderhook Creek also overflowed its banks, resulting in additional flash flooding.||As a result of the flooding, Amtrak train service was suspended early Thursday morning on July 30th between the Rensselaer Rail Station and Penn Station, in order for track and signal systems to be examined for flood damage in Hudson. The rail service resumed by early Thursday afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding occurred in central and southern Rensselaer County Wednesday afternoon and evening, as a result of training thunderstorms which produced excessive rainfall, and caused Kinderhook Creek to overflow its banks.||Numerous roadways and bridges were closed, some of which were washed out.  The hardest hit areas included, but were not limited to, Nassau, Stephentown, Schodack, and Sand Lake.||In Nassau, a state of emergency was declared, and State Route 43 between Pikes Pond Road and Reno Road was closed due to flooding.||In Stephentown, the bridge at State Route 22 and Provost Road was deemed unsafe, resulting in the closure of a portion of Route 22. In addition, flooding closed a portion of South Stephentown Road between Garfield Road and Andrews Lane.||In Sand Lake, Bauer Road was closed due to a bridge washout.||In Schodack, Clove Road off State Route 43 was closed to due flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass remained in place across the area Wednesday, July 1st. A strong upper level low over the Great Lakes Region with disturbances rotating about it triggered numerous thunderstorms across eastern New York during the afternoon and early evening hours, some of which became severe. ||In addition, some thunderstorms produced torrential downpours leading to flash flooding, especially in areas that received heavy rainfall on June 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of Route 2, Troy Schenectady Road in Latham was closed due to flash flooding from intense rainfall. A nearby business was also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system approached the region Thursday, July 16th. A warm front associated with the low moved northeast of the region by early afternoon, placing the local area in a very unstable airmass. A cold front then moved across the area Thursday night. In addition, an upper level low was approaching from the Great Lakes Region. Severe thunderstorms developed across Herkimer County by mid afternoon and moved southeastward across east central New York through the rest of the afternoon into the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 64 mph was reported in Port Ewen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure moved eastward along a stalled frontal boundary across the area as a strong upper level disturbance passed over region, aiding in the development of showers and thunderstorms across east central New York during the day Friday July 31st. The airmass in place was very humid, allowing storms to produce torrential downpours.  In addition, the storms were slow movers.  ||Generally, 1 to 3 inches of rain was reported across the area, with the highest totals across portions of Albany, Columbia, Rensselaer and Ulster counties. The ground was waterlogged and rivers and streams were already running high from the torrential rains which fell in the couple of days prior. Northern Columbia and southern Rensselaer counties were hard hit by flash flooding on Wednesday, so the heavy rain on Friday resulted in additional flash flooding and damage. ||States of Emergency which were declared across portions of northern Columbia and southern Rensselaer counties remained in effect into Friday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mountainview Street and Helderberg Parkway, also known as Route 85A, in Voorheesville were closed due to flash flooding from very heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a slow moving cold front approaching from the west, and an abundantly moist and warm air mass in place, led to the development of numerous showers and thunderstorms across eastern New York and western New England during Wednesday afternoon and evening on July 29th.||Some of the thunderstorms were severe, producing isolated damaging winds.  In addition, several clusters of thunderstorms moved slowly, and trained across the same areas for several hours, producing torrential rainfall and resulting in significant flash flooding.||Trained spotters measured rainfall amounts of 4 to 7 inches within the most persistent thunderstorms over a 3 to 6 hour period, particularly near Kinderhook, and adjacent areas of northern Columbia and southern Rensselaer Counties. In addition to widespread flash flooding, the Kinderhook Creek also overflowed its banks, resulting in additional flash flooding.||As a result of the flooding, Amtrak train service was suspended early Thursday morning on July 30th between the Rensselaer Rail Station and Penn Station, in order for track and signal systems to be examined for flood damage in Hudson. The rail service resumed by early Thursday afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down approximately 1 mile north of Woodstock due to lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rather complex weather pattern was in place across the region Friday, July 17th, along with an unstable airmass. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, humid and unstable airmass was in place as a weakening cold front moved across the area Monday afternoon, July 27th. In addition, a strong upper level disturbance moved over the region, helping to trigger thunderstorms across the mid Hudson Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure moved eastward along a stalled frontal boundary across the area as a strong upper level disturbance passed over region, aiding in the development of showers and thunderstorms across east central New York during the day Friday July 31st. The airmass in place was very humid, allowing storms to produce torrential downpours.  In addition, the storms were slow movers.  ||Generally, 1 to 3 inches of rain was reported across the area, with the highest totals across portions of Albany, Columbia, Rensselaer and Ulster counties. The ground was waterlogged and rivers and streams were already running high from the torrential rains which fell in the couple of days prior. Northern Columbia and southern Rensselaer counties were hard hit by flash flooding on Wednesday, so the heavy rain on Friday resulted in additional flash flooding and damage. ||States of Emergency which were declared across portions of northern Columbia and southern Rensselaer counties remained in effect into Friday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 4 and Route 32 were closed in Stillwater due to flash flooding from very heavy rainfall.

Severe thunderstorms developed across Herkimer County by mid afternoon and moved southeastward across east central New York through the rest of the afternoon into the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing over the intersection of White House Crossing Road and Route 22, near Boston Corner, blocking traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass remained in place across the area Wednesday, July 1st. A strong upper level low over the Great Lakes Region with disturbances rotating about it triggered numerous thunderstorms across eastern New York during the afternoon and early evening hours, some of which became severe. ||In addition, some thunderstorms produced torrential downpours leading to flash flooding, especially in areas that received heavy rainfall on June 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed due to flash flooding from very heavy rainfall in Schenectady including portions of Weaver Street, Jay Street, Union Street and Broadway. Vehicles were stuck in the flood waters. At least one person had to be rescued after getting trapped on the roof of her minivan under the Weaver Street bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a slow moving cold front approaching from the west, and an abundantly moist and warm air mass in place, led to the development of numerous showers and thunderstorms across eastern New York and western New England during Wednesday afternoon and evening on July 29th.||Some of the thunderstorms were severe, producing isolated damaging winds.  In addition, several clusters of thunderstorms moved slowly, and trained across the same areas for several hours, producing torrential rainfall and resulting in significant flash flooding.||Trained spotters measured rainfall amounts of 4 to 7 inches within the most persistent thunderstorms over a 3 to 6 hour period, particularly near Kinderhook, and adjacent areas of northern Columbia and southern Rensselaer Counties. In addition to widespread flash flooding, the Kinderhook Creek also overflowed its banks, resulting in additional flash flooding.||As a result of the flooding, Amtrak train service was suspended early Thursday morning on July 30th between the Rensselaer Rail Station and Penn Station, in order for track and signal systems to be examined for flood damage in Hudson. The rail service resumed by early Thursday afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires and a pole were reported down near Kinderhook due to strong thunderstorm winds.
